David Chalmers, along with Frank Jackson and others, played a major role in developing two-dimensional semantics.
Frank Jackson defended epiphenomenalism in contemporary philosophy, although he later changed his stance to physicalism.
Frank Jackson authored the article 'What Mary didn’t Know,' published in the Journal of Philosophy in 1986.
Frank Jackson authored the paper 'Mind and Illusion', published in the Royal Institute of Philosophy Supplements in 2003.

Frank Jackson (1982) argues that mental states are evolutionary by-products of brain states, using the analogy that the heaviness of a polar bear's coat is a side effect of the coat's warmth-providing function.

The author argues that Frank Jackson's analogy of evolutionary by-products fails for mental states because the laws of physics governing brain states do not explain why those brain states produce conscious experiences.

Frank Jackson's hypothetical scenario of Mary, the super color scientist, illustrates the claim that third-person theory has limits, specifically regarding what can be understood about experiencing red without having had that experience.
Frank Jackson (1993) notes that it is controversial whether a priori deducibility of the explanandum from the explanans is a necessary or sufficient criterion for explaining consciousness.
Frank Jackson expressed a contrary view to his earlier knowledge argument in his 1998 and 2004 publications.

Frank Jackson (2004) has charged non-reductive physicalism with incoherence because it attempts to claim physical realization while denying the ability to explain that relation in a strict and a priori intelligible way.
Interferon-alpha (IFN-α) is a pro-inflammatory cytokine produced by macrophages, neutrophils, and some somatic cells that binds to CD118 (IFNAR1, IFNAR2) and functions as an anti-viral agent.
Interferon-beta (IFN-β) is a pro-inflammatory cytokine produced by fibroblasts that binds to CD118 (IFNAR1, IFNAR2) and functions as an anti-viral and anti-proliferative agent.

Judit Gervain, a professor of developmental and social psychology at the University of Padua, researches early speech perception and language acquisition in monolingual and bilingual infants using behavioral and brain imaging techniques.

The author of 'Consciousness and Cognitive Sciences' asserts that the development of new cerebral imaging techniques has a paradoxical effect on how the psychophenomenological level is considered in research.
Brain imaging studies suggest that multilingualism influences brain structure and function, which may impact cognitive flexibility and perspective-taking abilities.

The study 'Brain imaging reveals covert consciousness during behavioral unresponsiveness induced by propofol' published in Scientific Reports demonstrates that brain imaging can detect covert consciousness in patients who are behaviorally unresponsive due to propofol.
Dr. Ragini Varma at The University of Pennsylvania used brain imaging techniques to confirm that the brains of men and women are 'hard-wired' differently.
Cognitive science employs experiments, brain imaging, and computer models to explore the processes of knowledge acquisition and information processing.
Cognitive science combines empirical research, such as experiments and brain imaging, with theoretical modeling, such as computational models.
Researchers can utilize brain imaging techniques, such as fMRI or CT scans, to determine how the deactivation or activation of specific brain regions affects behavior.
A subset of 37,898 individuals from the UK Biobank cohort underwent multi-modal brain imaging to explore the relationship between sleep and brain volume.

New scientific techniques in advertising research, such as brain wave measurement, brain image scans, facial response analysis, eye-tracking, heart rate monitoring, skin conductance measurement, and response latency, claim to reveal how advertising exerts influence on the unconscious mind.

He et al. (2021) reviewed 105 consumer neuroscience studies that utilized brain imaging techniques and proposed a framework emphasizing a multi-brain perspective, using databases including WOS, BCI, CSCD, DIIDW, FSTA, KJD, MEDLINE, RSCI, and SCIELO for the period 2000–2020.

Neuromarketing is a field that combines neuroscience, psychology, and marketing to gain insights into consumer behavior at a neurological level using techniques like brain imaging and biometric measurements.
The integumentary system provides exterior protection of the body and thermal regulation, utilizing skin, hair, fat, and nails.
The hypodermis, also called the underlying fascia, consists of fat, connective tissue, and skin appendages such as hair, nails, and sebaceous and sweat glands.
The human body exhibits mammalian characteristics, including the presence of hair, mammary glands, and highly developed sense organs.
Extracellular materials in the human body, such as hair and nails, are composed of protein.

Proteins serve as major structural components of the human body, forming extracellular materials like hair and nails, as well as collagen, which constitutes much of the skin, bones, tendons, and ligaments.
The Integumentary System encloses internal body structures and serves as the site of many sensory receptors; it consists of hair, skin, and nails.
Fluctuating hormone levels throughout the menstrual cycle can cause changes in hair, skin, digestion, mood, migraine headaches, and sexual experience.

During puberty, testosterone facilitates physical changes in boys, including growth of the penis and testes, development of facial, pubic, and body hair, deepening of the voice, increased muscle and bone strength, and height growth.

The NHS and other sources categorize the Paleo diet as a 'fad diet' that promises effortless weight loss, similar to other diets like vegan, Mediterranean, low fat, low carb, raw food, and gluten-free diets.
Humans have evolved to depend upon cooked food and cannot survive on raw, unprocessed food alone.
In an experiment conducted by Richard Wrangham and his students, mice raised on cooked foods gained 15 to 40 percent more weight than mice raised only on raw food.

A long-term raw food diet has consequences for body weight and menstruation, as reported in the 1999 study 'Consequences of a Long-Term Raw Food Diet on Body Weight and Menstruation: Results of a Questionnaire Survey' published in Annals of Nutrition and Metabolism.
D. S. Clarke compiled 'Panpsychism: Past and Recent Selected Readings', published by the State University of New York Press in 2004.
D.S. Clarke edited 'Panpsychism: Past and Recent Selected Readings', a collection of texts on the history and recent developments of panpsychism.
Cultures with low uncertainty avoidance are more flexible and open to new ideas, which often leads to the development of more innovative communication strategies.
Cultures with low uncertainty avoidance are more flexible and open to new ideas, which often leads to more innovative communication strategies, according to Wang & Lee (2023).
Bacterial and viral infections can cause enteritis, which is inflammation of the small intestine.

Bacterial infections like strep throat and viral infections like the flu can cause throat inflammation, while other bacterial and viral infections can cause inflammation of the small intestine known as enteritis.

family.name: The blank slate: the modern denial of human nature

facts.content: Steven Pinker authored 'The blank slate: the modern denial of human nature' in 2002, which addresses the concept of human nature.
Evolutionary psychologist Steven Pinker delivered a TED talk regarding his book, "The Blank Slate: The Modern Denial of Human Nature."
Steven Pinker's book 'The Blank Slate: The Modern Denial of Human Nature' (2002) provides an accessible introduction to the ideas of evolutionary psychology.

Feedforward regulation, also known as central command, is a mechanism of homeostasis where a response is elicited without feedback about the status of the regulated variable, allowing adjustments to be made before changes in the regulated variable have occurred.

Homeostatic control is organized as a hierarchical, multi-level, multi-goal seeking system consisting of three levels: physiochemical processes (first level), autonomous self-regulation such as the baroreceptor reflex (second level), and central command and control centers in the central nervous system (third level).

Physiological reflex adjustments during exercise are contributed to by both feedback mechanisms (sensory information from the exercising muscle, known as the exercise pressor reflex) and feedforward mechanisms (central command, such as the anticipation of exercise onset).
In the Paradigm of Allostatic Orchestration, the brain acts as the organ of central command, orchestrating cross-system operations to support optimal behavior at the level of the whole organism.

Chronic stress, or 'assaults on the brain,' are predicted to impact all physiological subsystems regulated by the central command, even when the stressors originate from non-physical sources such as mental, social, or cultural factors.

Evaluation techniques such as 'LLM-as-a-judge' or 'TLM' (Trustworthy Language Model) can be powered by any Large Language Model and do not require specific data preparation, labeling, or custom model infrastructure, provided the user has infrastructure to run pre-trained LLMs like AWS Bedrock, Azure/OpenAI, Gemini, or Together.ai.
Saab et al. (2024) published 'Capabilities of gemini models in medicine' as an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2404.18416).

Deep learning neural network-based large language models, such as GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini, process unstructured data including text, images, video, and streaming sensor data to learn patterns, classify data, and make predictions.
Large Language Models (LLMs) are transformer-based language models, including OpenAI’s GPT-4, Google’s Gemini and PaLM, Microsoft’s Phi-3, and Meta’s LLaMA.
The Gemini Team published a technical report on the Gemini family of multimodal models as an arXiv preprint in 2023.

Whitfield S and Yang S authored 'Evaluating AI Language Models for Reference Services: A Comparative Study of ChatGPT, Gemini, and Copilot', published in Internet Reference Services Quarterly in 2025.
Asiri (2025) assessed the reliability of ChatGPT and Gemini in identifying relevant orthodontic literature, published in the European Journal of General Dentistry.

Birinci M, Kilictas A, Gül O, Yemiş T, Erdivanlı B, Çeliker M, Özgür A, Çelebi Erdivanlı Ö, and Dursun E authored 'Large Language Models for Cochlear Implant Education: A Comparison of ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and DeepSeek', published in Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery in 2026.
When evaluating hallucination detection capabilities, Gemini correctly detected hallucination types but did not follow instructions well, providing extensive explanations for its classifications.
The MediHallDetector model surpasses GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and Gemini in hallucination detection performance and improves efficiency compared to manual evaluation, though it still trails human performance.
Prominent large language models include OpenAI’s GPT series, Google’s Gemini, Anthropic’s Claude, and Meta’s Llama family.

The most commonly mentioned AI/LLM tools by survey respondents were ChatGPT (30 mentions), followed by Claude (20), Google Bard/Gemini (16), Llama (15), Perplexity (9), Alphafold (2), and Scite and Consensus (1).
The models gemini-2.0 and deepseek-r1 demonstrate robust hallucination resistance, positioning themselves alongside o1-preview and outperforming earlier models.

The highest-performing models, including gemini-2.0-thinking, gemini-2.0, and deepseek-r1, cluster in the high similarity score range of 0.7-0.9, indicating a strong semantic alignment of their outputs with ground truth medical information.
DeepMind’s AlphaProof integrates an AlphaZero-style planner with a Gemini-class large language model to solve Olympiad-level mathematical proofs while adhering to formal soundness guarantees.

Team G, Anil R, Borgeaud S, Wu Y, Alayrac J-B, Yu J, Soricut R, Schalkwyk J, Dai AM, Hauth A et al. authored 'Gemini: a family of highly capable multimodal models', published as an arXiv preprint in 2023.

Large Language Models such as ChatGPT (OpenAI, 2022), DeepSeek (Guo et al., 2025), Qwen (Bai et al., 2023a), Llama (Touvron et al., 2023), Gemini (Team et al., 2023), and Claude (Caruccio et al., 2024) have transcended the boundaries of traditional Natural Language Processing as established by Vaswani et al. (2017a).
The paper 'Gemini: a family of highly capable multimodal models' is an arXiv preprint, arXiv:2312.11805.
Multimodal Large Language Models, such as Google's Gemini and GPT-4 with vision (GPT-4V), possess vision capabilities.
Future iterations of the HalluLens benchmark could be strengthened by including diverse models such as Gemini and incorporating human raters.

Studies by Maina et al. identify persistent challenges in LLM-as-a-Judge methods, including verbosity bias, inconsistency in low-resource languages, and a 'severity gap' where models like GPT-5 and Gemini exhibit divergent leniency compared to human clinicians.

The Majority Voting strategy for ensemble LLM judges consistently produces stable agreement with human clinical experts, maintaining F1-scores in the 75–79% range across Doctor Agents including DeepSeek, Gemini, and GPT-5.
MedGemma is a specialized open-source multimodal model built on the Gemma 3 architecture, incorporating research and technology derived from Google’s proprietary Gemini models.
Over 75% of professionals use artificial intelligence tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Copilot, or Claude in their daily work for writing and editing messages.

Cancer treatments that can damage the thyroid gland and lead to hypothyroidism include radiation to the head and neck, thyroidectomy, total body irradiation, high doses of chemotherapy, certain types of immunotherapy and targeted therapy, and radioactive iodine.
Cancer treatment can damage specific endocrine organs: radiation to the head and neck may damage the thyroid, while radiation to the pelvis may damage the ovaries in women or the testes in men.

The paper 'The Influence of Behavioral Biases on Investment Decisions' draws conceptual linkages between knowledge hiding, a concept typically studied in organizational contexts by Jena & Swain (2021), and information asymmetry in retail finance ecosystems.
Mr Swain compares Steven Greer to Billy Meier in the context of UFO contact claims.
Mr Swain characterizes Steven Greer's claims regarding extraterrestrial contact as "BS" and compares him to Billy Meier.
Dr. Steven M. Greer earned a Doctor of Medicine from the James H. Quillen College of Medicine at East Tennessee State University in 1987.
Steven Greer obtained a medical degree from East Tennessee State University.
Dr. Steven M. Greer earned a Doctor of Medicine from East Tennessee State University, James H. Quillen College of Medicine in 1987.
An irreverent tone deliberately displays a lack of respect or seriousness, often utilized in satirical or controversial writing.

The intended audience influences the tone of a piece of writing: professional audiences often expect formal or respectful tones; younger audiences may prefer conversational or irreverent tones; audiences familiar with a topic may appreciate specialized or cerebral tones; and general audiences are better served by clear and straightforward tones.
In 2021, a WTO panel ruled against the People's Republic of China regarding its challenge to U.S. solar tariffs, and the People's Republic of China appealed the ruling to the defunct Appellate Body.
In 2022, a USMCA panel ruled in favor of Canada regarding its challenge to U.S. solar tariffs, leading Canada to reach a mutually agreed settlement with the United States.
Nostradamus is popularly known for writing quatrains that are purported to predict important events, though the specific meaning of these quatrains varies depending on how they are translated.

The speaker on the 'Stuff They Don't Want You To Know' podcast identifies a specific quatrain often attributed to Nostradamus—'in nine hundred and nineties six build your house with wooden sticks for war and fire will sweep over the land'—as actually being lyrics from the story of the Three Little Pigs.
Nostradamus is popularly known for writing quatrains that are interpreted as predictions of important events, though the accuracy of these predictions depends heavily on how the texts are translated.

A specific quatrain often attributed to Nostradamus, which mentions building a house with wooden sticks and fire sweeping over the land, is actually a misattributed excerpt from the story 'The Three Little Pigs'.

Historical figures identified as channels include Moses, Muhammad, Merlin, Nostradamus, and Emanuel Swedenborg, with channeling practices dating back thousands of years to aboriginal shamanic traditions.
The 'Conversations With Nostradamus' series (volumes 1-3) by Dolores Cannon explores the prophecies of Nostradamus through hypnotic sessions.

Dolores Cannon claimed to establish communication with the prophet Nostradamus through her hypnosis subjects and published the three-volume set 'Conversations With Nostradamus' containing interpretations of nearly 1000 prophecies.
Dolores Cannon claimed to establish communication with the living Michel De Notredame, known as the prophet Nostradamus, while working with several different subjects during hypnosis sessions.

Dolores Cannon authored the three-volume set 'Conversations With Nostradamus', which contains the translation and interpretation of almost 1000 of Nostradamus' quatrains and has been in print since 1989.
Dolores Cannon's work is linked to the interpretation of Nostradamus's prophecies, specifically regarding the fire at Notre Dame on April 15, 2019.
Dolores Cannon is widely acknowledged as a leading authority on Nostradamus and is considered the world's foremost expert on his prophecies.

The study 'Motivation of software developers in Open Source projects: an Internet-based survey of contributors to the Linux kernel' published in Research Policy (vol 32, issue 7) investigates the motivations of Linux kernel contributors.

The authors of the study derive four policy recommendations categorized into economic and development policies, just energy transition policies, and research policy, which target stakeholders including development agencies, national governments of the global North, hydrogen diplomats, political decision-makers, and research funding agencies.
Gebeyehu (2020) conducted an ethnobotanical study of medicinal plants used to treat human and livestock ailments in the Entoto forest and its environment in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia.
The Goba District is located in the Kafa Zone of the Southwest Ethiopia Regional State, approximately 64 km from Bonga and southwest of Addis Ababa.
Sedie Muja District is located 772 km from Addis Ababa, the capital city of Ethiopia.
I. Hedberg, I. Friss, and E. Persson published 'Flora of Ethiopia and Eritrea' (Volume 8) in 2009 through The National Herbarium in Addis Ababa and Uppsala.

Zemede, A. presented 'The Future of Wild Food Plants in Southern Ethiopia: Ecosystem Conservation Coupled with Enhancement of the Roles of Key Social Groups' at the Symposium on Underutilized Plants for Food Security in Ethiopia in Addis Ababa on January 31, 2009.
Mieso District is situated 300 km southeast of Addis Ababa, the capital city of Ethiopia.

Plant species identification was performed in the field and at the national herbarium using taxonomic literature, comparison with voucher specimen references, and flora books of Ethiopia and Eritrea. Specimens were pressed, labeled, and deposited in the National Herbarium in Addis Ababa and Oda Bultum University.

family.name: Handbook on effective police responses to violence against women

facts.content: The United Nations created the 'Handbook on effective police responses to violence against women' to provide guidelines for managing violence through effective laws, law enforcement policies, and community activities aimed at breaking down societal norms that condone violence.

The United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) published the 'Handbook on effective police responses to violence against women' in 2010, which addresses justice system responses, victim protection, and the role of the police.

Lepidopteran abundance and richness were depressed on both congener and unrelated non-congener non-native plants over a two-year study, with the effect being more pronounced on non-congener non-native plants.
Only 7% of specialist lepidopteran species used non-congener non-native plants as hosts.
Anil Seth argues that treating entities that appear conscious as if they are not conscious is psychologically harmful to humans, citing arguments dating back to Immanuel Kant.
Immanuel Kant denied that introspection presents humans with any sort of self-as-object, echoing an insight previously held by David Hume.

Immanuel Kant argued that it is a condition of all thought that it be self-conscious in the sense of being attributed to a thinker, meaning one cannot think without thinking that one is oneself thinking.
Immanuel Kant wrote in the Critique of pure reason: “the ‘I think’ must be able to accompany all my representations.”

Immanuel Kant introduced the idea that self-consciousness is interdependent with consciousness of an external objective world, asserting that one can only be aware of oneself to the extent that one experiences an organized world of objects that interact causally and predictably.
During the early modern period, self-consciousness became a central topic in epistemology and the philosophy of mind, particularly through the work of Immanuel Kant and the post-Kantians.
Immanuel Kant argues that there is no intuition of the self through which the self is given as an object.
Immanuel Kant claims that transcendental apperception, a form of self-awareness, is required to account for the unity of conscious experience over time.
According to Immanuel Kant, the representation of the self in the 'I think' is purely formal and is exhausted by its function in unifying experience.
Arthur Schopenhauer concurred with Immanuel Kant's view that the subject becoming an object for itself is a contradiction.

The 'Heidelberg School' interprets Johann Gottlieb Fichte as claiming that previous accounts of self-consciousness by René Descartes, John Locke, and Immanuel Kant are 'reflective' because they regard the self as an object rather than a subject.
Edmund Husserl initially denied the inner awareness of a 'pure ego' in his early work, but later revised this view to resemble Immanuel Kant's transcendental apperception.

Immanuel Kant's First Critique (1781/1787) utilizes the claim that there is no conscious awareness of the self in arguments such as the Transcendental Deduction, the Refutation of Idealism, and the Paralogisms.

P.F. Strawson, in his discussion of Immanuel Kant's transcendental deduction, articulates the claim that if different experiences are to belong to a single consciousness, the subject of those experiences must have the possibility of self-consciousness.

Immanuel Kant argues that a subject must be able to comprehend their manifold representations in a single consciousness to call them their own, otherwise the self would be as diverse as the representations themselves.
Immanuel Kant's 'Critique of Pure Reason' (1781/1787) was edited by Paul Guyer and Alan Wood and published by Cambridge University Press in 1997.
C. Thomas Powell analyzed Immanuel Kant's theory of self-consciousness in his 1990 book 'Kant’s Theory of Self-Consciousness'.
P.F. Strawson analyzed Immanuel Kant's philosophy in the 1966 book 'The Bounds of Sense'.

The mind-body problem became the central question of epistemology and modern philosophy due to the problematization of the mind and its relation to reality by René Descartes, John Locke, George Berkeley, David Hume, and Immanuel Kant.
George Berkeley, David Hume, and Immanuel Kant contested the possibility of acquiring reliable knowledge about the physical world.

von Stillfried (2018) argues that David Chalmers' definition of consciousness is circular because, if experience is assumed to be the only intrinsic evidence, the causal structure of time-space cannot be differentiated from cognitive structures, a problem previously demonstrated by David Hume and Immanuel Kant.
David Chalmers paraphrases Immanuel Kant to describe the relationship between the hard and easy problems of consciousness: 'hard without easy is empty; easy without hard is blind.'

Arthur Schopenhauer (1788–1860) theorized that 'will' is the inner nature of all things, based on his introspection of will as a fundamental quality, while being influenced by Immanuel Kant's view that reality as it is in itself is inaccessible.

The 'no-summing argument' against panpsychism contends that combining conscious subjects is logically impossible because conscious subjects are inherently indivisible unities, a concept historically associated with Leibniz and Kant.

Immanuel Kant critiqued the purely associationist approach to consciousness in 1787, arguing that an adequate account of experience and phenomenal consciousness requires a complex structure of mental and intentional organization.

Immanuel Kant argued that phenomenal consciousness cannot be a mere succession of associated ideas, but must be the experience of a conscious self situated in an objective world structured by space, time, and causality.

Immanuel Kant (1787), Edmund Husserl (1913), and subsequent phenomenologists demonstrated that the phenomenal structure of experience is intentional and includes complex representations of time, space, cause, body, self, and the world.
Immanuel Kant (1787) noted that the "I think" must at least potentially accompany every conscious experience, even if the self does not appear as an explicit element within that experience.

Immanuel Kant (1787) and Edmund Husserl (1929) argued that the intentional coherence of the experiential domain relies on a dual interdependence between the self as a perspective and the world as an integrated structure of objects and events.

Immanuel Kant (1787) and Edmund Husserl (1913) argue that individual conscious experience depends on its location within a larger unified structure of representation, which includes awareness of one's existence as a temporally extended observer within a world of spatially connected objects.
Immanuel Kant synthesized the debate between Rationalism and Empiricism by recognizing the interplay between reason and experience in shaping human cognition.
Immanuel Kant bridged the gap between rationalism and empiricism by advocating that both reason and experience contribute to human knowledge.
Immanuel Kant is a key figure who bridged the views of rationalism and empiricism.
German philosopher Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argues that for perceptions to make sense, they must be received into concepts that already exist within the human mind.
According to Immanuel Kant, transcendental categories are innate structures or concepts that bridge the gap between the mind and the world, molding how humans experience reality.
Immanuel Kant's philosophical view, known as transcendental idealism, reconciles rationalism and empiricism by combining sensory input with inborn concepts to explain how humans understand the world.
Immanuel Kant argues that adequate knowledge requires a combination of both reason and experience.

Immanuel Kant defines 'analytic truths' as statements where the predicate term is contained within or is the meaning of the subject term, such as 'a circle is round' or 'a bachelor is an unmarried man'.
Immanuel Kant defines 'synthetic truths' as statements where the predicate term is neither contained within nor is the meaning of the subject term, meaning they are not true by definition.
Immanuel Kant maintains that only synthetic truths are capable of providing substantive new information about the world.

Immanuel Kant classified mathematical truths, such as the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter being greater than 3, as synthetic, contrary to the common classification of these truths as analytic.

Immanuel Kant claimed that Euclid’s axioms of geometry, basic features of space and time, metaphysical truths, and moral truths possess metaphysical necessity, meaning they hold universally and are eternal, even if they are contingent in a strict logical sense.
Immanuel Kant defined "synthesis" as "the act of putting different representations [elements of cognition] together, and grasping what is manifold in them in one cognition."

Immanuel Kant's process of synthesis consists of three sequential steps: (1) synthesis of apprehension in perception, (2) synthesis of reproduction in imagination, and (3) synthesis of recognition in a concept.

Immanuel Kant argues that apperception requires two types of unity: data must represent a common subject, and data must be held together by a unified self, referred to as the "unity of consciousness" or "unity of apperception."

Immanuel Kant stated in his 'Critique of Pure Reason': 'Up to now it has been assumed that all our cognition must conform to the objects; but all attempts to find out something about them a priori through concepts that would extend our cognition have, on this presupposition, come to nothing. Hence let us once try whether we do not get farther with the problems of metaphysics by assuming that the objects must conform to our cognition, which would agree better with the requested possibility of an a priori cognition of them, which is to establish something about objects before they are given to us. This would be just like the first thoughts of Copernicus, who, when he did not make good progress in the explanation of the celestial motions if he assumed that the entire celestial host revolves around the observer, tried to see if he might not have greater success if he made the observer revolve and left the stars at rest. Now in metaphysics we can try in a similar way regarding the intuition of objects. If intuition has to conform to the constitution of the objects, then I do not see how we can know anything of them a priori; but if the object (as an object of the senses) conforms to the constitution of our faculty of intuition, then I can very well represent this possibility to myself. Yet because I cannot stop with these intuitions, if they are to become cognitions, but must refer them as representations to something as their object and determine this object through them, I can assume either that the concepts through which I bring about this determination also conform to the objects, and then I am once again in the same difficulty about how I could know anything about them a priori, or else I assume that the objects, or what is the same thing, the experience in which alone they can be cognized (as given objects) conforms to those concepts, in which case I immediately see an easier way out of the difficulty, since experience itself is a kind of cognition requiring the understanding, whose rule I have to presuppose in myself before any object is given to me, hence a priori, which rule is expressed in concepts a priori, to which all objects of experience must therefore necessarily conform, and with which they must agree.'
Post-Kantian philosophers interpreted Immanuel Kant's theory differently, which led to the analytic-continental divide in philosophy.
Some continental philosophers interpret Immanuel Kant as asserting that humans cannot know things as they are in themselves (the noumena), but only how they appear to us (the phenomena).
The interpretation of Immanuel Kant's theory regarding phenomena and noumena resulted in external-world skepticism, Edmund Husserl’s phenomenology, or a constructivist view of reality.
Immanuel Kant's synthesis of rationalism and empiricism utilizes a transcendental bridge between the mind and the world, which makes synthetic a priori knowledge possible.
The term 'idealism', when not preceded by 'transcendental', refers to the theories of George Berkeley or Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel, which are distinct from Immanuel Kant's view.
Immanuel Kant's theory of knowledge attempts to overcome the limitations of both rationalism and empiricism by merging their strengths into a coherent whole.
Immanuel Kant states that knowledge begins with experience (sensibility), proceeds through reason (categories), and ends in the mind (principles).
Immanuel Kant posits that human cognition relies on two essential powers: perceiving (sensibility) and understanding (reasoning), and that knowledge is impossible without both faculties.
Immanuel Kant argues that space, time, causation, quantity, and quality are represented to humans in innate structures or concepts that the mind is fitted with prior to experience.
Immanuel Kant argues that human sense experiences do not passively enter the mind but conform to innate, a priori mental structures to facilitate knowledge.
Immanuel Kant posits 'synthetic a priori' knowledge as a category of knowledge derived from a priori reflection on the structured nature of the world, such as space-time and cause-effect relations.
Immanuel Kant argued that human concepts discriminate and organize information received from the senses through acts of synthesis.
In Immanuel Kant's theory of cognition, the 'synthesis of apprehension in perception' involves locating an object in space and time.
In Immanuel Kant's theory of cognition, the 'synthesis of reproduction in imagination' consists of connecting different elements in the mind to form an image.

In Immanuel Kant's theory of cognition, the 'synthesis of recognition in a concept' requires memory of a past experience and recognizing its relation to a present experience to form a concept of an object.
Immanuel Kant defined 'apperception' as the attachment of meaning to percepts by recognizing something as a unified object under a concept.

Immanuel Kant concluded that humans are equally capable of making sense of the same public object in a uniform manner because of the 'unity of apperception,' which allows for shared synthetic knowledge of objective reality.

Immanuel Kant, in his work 'Critique of Pure Reason', draws an analogy between his epistemological theory and the Copernican Revolution in astronomy, which shifted the scientific model from geocentric to heliocentric.

Immanuel Kant argues that if intuition must conform to the constitution of objects, it is impossible to know anything about those objects a priori; however, if objects conform to the human faculty of intuition, a priori knowledge becomes possible.

Immanuel Kant posits that experience is a form of cognition that requires the understanding, and because the rules of understanding are presupposed in the human mind before any object is given, these rules are a priori and all objects of experience must necessarily conform to them.
Immanuel Kant defines the transcendental as that which is presupposed in and necessary for experience, serving as an a priori element that makes experience possible.
The term 'epistemology' can refer to the specific philosophical positions held by individual philosophers, such as Plato's epistemology or Immanuel Kant's epistemology.

Immanuel Kant sought a middle ground between rationalism and empiricism by identifying knowledge pertaining to principles that underlie and structure all experience, such as spatial and temporal relations and fundamental categories of understanding.
The distinction between types of knowledge came to prominence in the 17th century and acted as a crucial factor in the philosophies of David Hume and Immanuel Kant.
S.J. Dick authored 'Plurality of Worlds: The Origin of the Extraterrestrial Life Debate from Democritus to Kant', published in Cambridge by Cambridge University Press in 1982.
Immanuel Kant is considered the founder of classical German philosophy, which exerted a dominant influence on European philosophy during the 19th century.
Immanuel Kant posits that a moral agent is obligated to act morally based on respect for universal law, which he terms the categorical imperative.

The Formula of Universal Law, a component of Immanuel Kant's categorical imperative, states: “act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law without contradiction.”

The Formula of Humanity, a component of Immanuel Kant's categorical imperative, states: “Act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end but always at the same time as an end.”

Applying Immanuel Kant's Formula of Universal Law to the maxim 'all lenders will regard loans to be aimed at foreclosure' creates a contradiction in the conception of a loan, because the definition of a loan requires mutual interest based on the borrower's ability to repay.
Immanuel Kant defined the Formula of Universal Law as: "act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law without contradiction."

Immanuel Kant defined the Formula of Humanity as: "Act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end but always at the same time as an end."

The Vanderbilt University Department of English seminar on modernity investigates the histories of critique, narratives of secularization, and claims of secular modernity by reading authors including Baruch Spinoza, Immanuel Kant, Karl Marx, Walter Benjamin, Michel Foucault, Jacob Taubes, Sylvia Wynter, Talal Asad, and Saba Mahmood.

Evolutionary psychology research covers diverse topics including language, morality, emotions, parental investment, homicide, social coercion, rape, psychopathologies, landscape preferences, spatial abilities, and pregnancy sickness.

JonBenét Patricia Ramsey was a six-year-old child beauty pageant contestant who was reported missing from her family's home in Boulder, Colorado, on December 26, 1996, and was found dead in the basement later that same day.
The Boulder County Coroner's Office determined the cause of death for JonBenét Ramsey was asphyxia by strangulation associated with craniocerebral trauma from blunt force injury to the head.
A handwritten ransom note found in the Ramsey home demanded $118,000 for the safe return of JonBenét Ramsey, an amount roughly equivalent to the Christmas bonus received by her father, John Ramsey.

Unidentified male DNA recovered from JonBenét Ramsey's clothing and the crime scene did not match family members or known associates, which supported the theory of an intruder rather than family involvement.
As of early 2026, the Boulder Police Department continues to treat the killing of JonBenét Ramsey as an active investigation and a top priority.
JonBenét Patricia Ramsey was born on August 6, 1990, at Northside Hospital in Atlanta, Georgia.
John Bennett Ramsey and Patricia 'Patsy' Ramsey are the parents of JonBenét Ramsey.
Burke Ramsey, the older brother of JonBenét Ramsey, was born in 1987.
Patsy Ramsey began entering JonBenét Ramsey in child beauty pageants in 1995 when the child was five years old.
By the end of 1996, JonBenét Ramsey had participated in at least nine beauty pageant competitions.
On December 25, 1996, the Ramsey family celebrated Christmas at their home in Boulder, Colorado, where JonBenét Ramsey received a bicycle as a gift.
On the night of December 25, 1996, Patsy Ramsey carried her daughter JonBenét Ramsey to her second-floor bedroom after the family returned from an event.

John Ramsey and his son Burke Ramsey retired to their respective rooms shortly after the family returned home on the night of December 25, 1996, which the parents stated was the last time they saw JonBenét Ramsey alive.

The ransom note addressed to John Ramsey demanded $118,000 in cash for the safe return of JonBenét Ramsey, specifically requesting $100,000 in $100 bills, $10,000 in $20 bills, and $8,000 in $10 bills.
The ransom note threatened JonBenét Ramsey with beheading or execution by a group identifying themselves as 'S.B.T.C.' and warned John Ramsey against contacting the police or using voice alterations.
The ransom note found in the JonBenét Ramsey case was over 350 words long, which linguistic analysts considered atypical for a standard ransom demand.

Linguistic analysis identified grammatical inconsistencies in the JonBenét Ramsey ransom note, specifically the phrase 'and son of a bitch,' which resembles dialogue from films such as Speed or Dirty Harry.

Investigators observed that the ransom note in the JonBenét Ramsey case used authoritative phrasing that was atypical for kidnappers who lacked control over the victim, who was already deceased in the basement.
No handwriting match has led to charges in the JonBenét Ramsey case, despite persistent theories implicating family involvement based on evidentiary ties.

Alternative analyses have proposed links to non-family suspects in the JonBenét Ramsey case based on stylistic comparisons of the ransom note, though these theories remain unverified by official forensics.
Patsy Ramsey dialed 911 at 5:52 a.m. on the day of the discovery of JonBenét Ramsey's body.
Patsy Ramsey reported to Boulder Police dispatcher Kim Archuleta on December 26, 1996, that her six-year-old daughter, JonBenét Ramsey, had been kidnapped from their home at 755 15th Street.
A three-page ransom note demanding $118,000 for the safe return of JonBenét Ramsey was discovered on the kitchen staircase of the Ramsey home.

The initial police response to the JonBenét Ramsey kidnapping report treated the case as a kidnapping and prioritized the ransom note's demands over a comprehensive interior search, resulting in a cursory walkthrough that overlooked the basement wine cellar where the body was later concealed.
John Ramsey discovered the body of JonBenét Ramsey in the basement wine cellar of the Ramsey home, covered in a white blanket, bound with duct tape, and associated with a garrote.
The discovery of JonBenét Ramsey's body occurred over seven hours after the police arrived at the Ramsey residence.
Patsy Ramsey made a 911 call reporting her daughter, JonBenét Ramsey, missing at 5:52 a.m. on December 26, 1996.
John Ramsey discovered the body of JonBenét Ramsey shortly after 1:30 p.m. on December 26, 1996.

JonBenét Ramsey's body was discovered in a storage room adjacent to the wine cellar, a dimly lit area partially concealed behind a boiler and accessible via a latched door that initial Boulder Police patrols had overlooked.
JonBenét Ramsey's body was found covered by a white blanket and wearing a red sequined pageant dress, white tights, and size-12 underwear, which was larger than her typical size-6.

JonBenét Ramsey's body was found with duct tape covering her mouth, wrists loosely bound with white cord, and a garrote fashioned from the same cord material and a broken paintbrush handle from the home encircling her neck.

John Ramsey removed the duct tape from JonBenét Ramsey's mouth and carried her body upstairs to the living room, an action that investigators later noted disturbed potential trace evidence and compromised the chain of custody.

Family members, friends, and clergy moved freely through the Ramsey home for hours following the discovery of JonBenét Ramsey's body, potentially contaminating surfaces and items before crime scene technicians arrived.

Boulder County Coroner Dr. John E. Meyer conducted the autopsy of JonBenét Ramsey on December 27, 1996, and determined the cause of death to be asphyxia by strangulation associated with craniocerebral trauma, with the manner of death ruled as homicide.

The autopsy of JonBenét Ramsey revealed a severe linear, comminuted skull fracture measuring approximately 8.5 inches in length on the right side of the head, accompanied by a 7-by-4-inch contusion on the scalp, extensive subarachnoid and subdural hemorrhaging, and contusions spanning 8 inches on the right cerebral hemisphere.

Strangulation of JonBenét Ramsey was evidenced by a tight ligature formed from a white nylon cord knotted around the neck and attached to a broken paintbrush handle, which produced a circumferential furrow and associated petechial hemorrhages on the neck, conjunctivae, and facial skin.
JonBenét Ramsey's body exhibited abrasions on the right cheek, shoulder, lower back, and leg, which were consistent with possible defensive actions or restraint.

A genital examination of JonBenét Ramsey disclosed acute vaginal trauma, including abrasion and vascular congestion of the mucosal folds, and a 1-centimeter red-purple abrasion on the hymen at the 7 o'clock position.
Forensic pathologists have debated whether the vaginal injuries found on JonBenét Ramsey definitively indicate sexual penetration or prior chronic abuse.
Toxicology screening of JonBenét Ramsey's body detected no presence of ethanol, illicit drugs, or other substances.
The autopsy of JonBenét Ramsey did not establish a precise time of death, though the body exhibited full rigor mortis upon discovery at approximately 1:00 p.m.
The death of JonBenét Ramsey likely occurred several hours earlier than the discovery of her body on December 26, 1996, potentially late on December 25, 1996.
Forensic testing conducted in the late 1990s detected foreign DNA belonging to an unidentified male, mixed with blood, in the crotch area of JonBenét Ramsey's underwear.
The DNA profile identified as 'Unknown Male 1' was first developed from samples taken from JonBenét Ramsey's underwear in 1998 and 1999.
In 2003, additional trace DNA from the same unknown male identified in the 1990s was found on JonBenét Ramsey's clothing.

By 2008, touch DNA analysis revealed genetic material from the unknown male on the waistband of the long johns JonBenét Ramsey was wearing, as well as on her tights and potentially under her fingernails.
The touch DNA found on JonBenét Ramsey's clothing was consistent across samples and was not attributable to contamination from first responders or family members.
No semen was recovered from JonBenét Ramsey's body or the crime scene, despite autopsy indications of possible sexual assault evidenced by vaginal abrasion and inflammation.

Unidentified male DNA on JonBenét Ramsey's clothing and under her fingernails, recovered early and excluding the family, shifted investigation emphasis toward an intruder, prompting the investigation of about 140 potential outsiders.

The presence of foreign male DNA in intimate areas like the underwear waistband of JonBenét Ramsey supports the theory of direct involvement by a non-family perpetrator, though critics argue it could represent incidental transfer.
Handwriting analysis commissioned by the police showed similarities between the JonBenét Ramsey ransom note and the handwriting of Patsy Ramsey, though no definitive match was confirmed.

Proponents of the theory that the JonBenét Ramsey killing was an inside job cite the absence of forced entry, the family's immediate retention of lawyers, and John Ramsey's discovery of the body in the basement after a police search as indicators of insider involvement.

The 2016 CBS documentary 'The Case Of: JonBenét Ramsey' popularized a theory that nine-year-old Burke Ramsey struck JonBenét Ramsey with a flashlight during a sibling dispute over a snack, causing a fatal head injury, and that the parents subsequently staged a garrote strangulation and ransom note to cover up the incident.

Circumstantial evidence cited in the theory that Burke Ramsey killed JonBenét Ramsey includes undigested pineapple in JonBenét's stomach matching a bowl in the kitchen containing Burke's fingerprints, Burke's calm demeanor during the crisis, and a prior incident where Burke struck JonBenét with a golf club.

Investigators in the 2016 CBS documentary 'The Case Of: JonBenét Ramsey', including former FBI profiler Bob Miller, contended that John and Patsy Ramsey obstructed the investigation to protect their son, Burke Ramsey.

Critics of the theory that the Ramsey family was involved in the killing of JonBenét Ramsey emphasize the presence of touch DNA from an unknown male found on JonBenét's long johns, underwear, and under her fingernails, which excluded all Ramsey family members.

Boulder District Attorney Mary Lacy formally exonerated the Ramsey family in 2008, stating that the DNA evidence found on JonBenét Ramsey's clothing and fingernails provided decisive evidence of an intruder.

Physical traces, including a Hi-Tec boot print in the basement and a broken basement window with intact cobwebs, contradict the theory that the Ramsey family staged the crime scene in the killing of JonBenét Ramsey.

The theory that Burke Ramsey was involved in the killing of JonBenét Ramsey lacks forensic linkage and has been dismissed by Ramsey attorneys as speculative defamation, leading to lawsuits against CBS.
Investigator Lou Smit identified wounds on JonBenét Ramsey, such as stun gun-like marks, that he argued were consistent with an intruder, while rejecting cover-up narratives as unsubstantiated.
No member of the Ramsey family has ever been charged in the killing of JonBenét Ramsey, and DNA evidence has excluded them as suspects.

According to the semiotic framework established by Charles Sanders Peirce, meaning emerges through a triadic relation among the representamen (the sign), the object (what the sign refers to), and the interpretant (the effect or understanding the sign produces).
Peircean semiotics posits that meaning is not inscribed in a sign itself but unfolds in the relation among the representamen, the object, and the interpretant.

Domínguez-Rodrigo, M., Pickering, T. R., Semaw, S., and Rogers, M. J. (2005) analyzed cutmarked bones from Pliocene archaeological sites at Gona, Afar, Ethiopia, to determine the function of the world’s oldest stone tools, as published in the Journal of Human Evolution 48, 109–121.

Earth's surface and atmosphere re-emit absorbed solar energy as heat in the form of longwave or infrared radiation.
Absorbed solar energy is re-emitted from the Earth's surface as heat in the form of longwave or infrared radiation.
The Earth radiates absorbed solar energy back into space as infrared radiation, but greenhouse gases absorb some of this outgoing heat, which leads to warming.

Low clouds emit nearly the same amount of infrared radiation as the Earth's surface due to their similar temperature, while high cold clouds emit less energy because of their low temperature, which hinders the Earth's capacity to cool and raises the planet's temperature.
Approximately 50% of solar radiation reaching the Earth's atmosphere is absorbed by the air and clouds and radiated as infrared heat.

Approximately 90% of the infrared radiation emitted from the Earth's surface is absorbed by greenhouse gases and re-emitted by the atmosphere in all directions, causing the Earth's surface and lower atmosphere to warm.

Global warming, defined as the constant rise in surface and ocean temperatures worldwide, is caused by the buildup of CO2 in the atmosphere, which traps infrared radiation released from the Earth's surface.

COSPAR Policy Categories III, IV, and V apply to missions targeting bodies of interest for chemical evolution or the origin of life, where there is a significant chance of contamination that could compromise future investigations.

The COSPAR Policy on Planetary Protection (Fisk et al., 2021) mandates that mission reports include specific data points: estimated bioburden at launch, methods used to obtain that estimate, bioburden composition for Category IV and Category V restricted Earth return missions, decontamination methods, organic inventory for components exceeding 1 kg, intended distance from target bodies, orbital parameters, and end-of-mission disposition.

Mitigating climate change involves reducing greenhouse gas emissions from electricity generation and converting transportation, industrial, and building energy consumption sectors to utilize electricity with lower greenhouse gas emissions.
From the early 1970s until approximately 2010, U.S. electricity generation and carbon dioxide emissions from the electricity sector increased in tandem.

The Boulder Police Department faced intense scrutiny for investigative missteps in the JonBenét Ramsey case, specifically regarding the failure to adequately secure the crime scene and the occurrence of early media leaks that fueled public suspicion toward John and Patsy Ramsey.
In 1999, a grand jury issued indictments against John and Patsy Ramsey for child endangerment and obstruction, though these charges were not pursued by the district attorney.
Boulder District Attorney Mary Lacy formally exonerated John and Patsy Ramsey in 2008, citing forensic evidence that was incompatible with their culpability.
FBI analysis determined that the handwriting on the ransom note found in the Ramsey home did not match the handwriting of Patsy Ramsey.
John Bennett Ramsey and Patricia 'Patsy' Ramsey are the parents of JonBenét Ramsey.
Patsy Ramsey began entering JonBenét Ramsey in child beauty pageants in 1995 when the child was five years old.
Patsy Ramsey earned a degree from West Virginia University.
On the night of December 25, 1996, Patsy Ramsey carried her daughter JonBenét Ramsey to her second-floor bedroom after the family returned from an event.
Patsy Ramsey discovered a ransom note on the back staircase of the Ramsey home early on December 26, 1996, and reported finding it shortly before calling 911 at 5:52 a.m.
A legal pad containing an indented prior practice sheet that matched the phrasing of the ransom note was found on Patsy Ramsey's desk.

The Colorado Bureau of Investigation and other experts analyzed the ransom note's handwriting and identified similarities in letter formation, spacing, and shading between the note and Patsy Ramsey's samples, but the results were deemed inconclusive for a definitive match or elimination.
Patsy Ramsey dialed 911 at 5:52 a.m. on the day of the discovery of JonBenét Ramsey's body.
Patsy Ramsey reported to Boulder Police dispatcher Kim Archuleta on December 26, 1996, that her six-year-old daughter, JonBenét Ramsey, had been kidnapped from their home at 755 15th Street.

In the 911 call, Patsy Ramsey described the ransom note as being addressed to her husband, John Ramsey, from a 'small foreign faction' and mentioned that her nine-year-old son, Burke Ramsey, was asleep upstairs.

Boulder Police dispatcher Kim Archuleta testified that Patsy Ramsey's tone during the 911 call shifted from hysterical and panicked to composed and scripted, leading Archuleta to keep the line open after she believed Patsy had disconnected.

After the 911 call appeared to disconnect, Kim Archuleta reported hearing faint background noises, including Patsy Ramsey saying 'Help me Jesus' twice, a muffled male voice stating 'We're not speaking to you,' and sounds interpreted as two additional individuals nearby.
The 911 call made by Patsy Ramsey on December 26, 1996, lasted approximately three minutes and 33 seconds.
Patsy Ramsey made a 911 call reporting her daughter, JonBenét Ramsey, missing at 5:52 a.m. on December 26, 1996.
Subsequent testing of the 'Unknown Male 1' DNA profile excluded John Ramsey, Patsy Ramsey, Burke Ramsey, and other individuals who had handled the body or been in contact with the clothing.
The garrote used to strangle JonBenét Ramsey consisted of a white nylon cord twisted around a broken handle from a paintbrush sourced from Patsy Ramsey's art supplies.
The Boulder Police Department received a 911 call from Patsy Ramsey at 5:52 a.m.
Patsy Ramsey reported her daughter JonBenét missing on December 26, 1996, and referenced a discovered ransom note demanding $118,000.
Boulder Police failed to separate John and Patsy Ramsey for formal interviews immediately on December 26, 1996.
Boulder Police conducted preliminary interviews with John and Patsy Ramsey for over two hours on December 26, 1996.

Formal interviews with John and Patsy Ramsey occurred on April 30, 1997, over four months after the crime, with Patsy Ramsey questioned for six and a half hours by two police investigators and a prosecutor regarding the ransom note's authorship, family dynamics, and potential intruders.

John Ramsey was interviewed by police on May 1, 1997, where he emphasized the ransom note's demands and his business connections as possible motives, while both John and Patsy Ramsey maintained that an intruder had entered the home through an unlocked basement window.

On June 24, 1997, the Boulder district attorney's investigators conducted additional sessions with John Ramsey, Patsy Ramsey, and Burke Ramsey, probing inconsistencies in timelines and Burke Ramsey's potential knowledge of the home's layout.
Police interviews conducted in 1998 and 2000 revisited topics including Patsy Ramsey's handwriting similarities to the ransom note, though no charges resulted from these interviews.

Boulder police named John and Patsy Ramsey as suspects by mid-1997, driven by the crime scene's contamination, the unusual ransom note made from household materials, and the family's delayed cooperation.
John and Patsy Ramsey were not subpoenaed to testify before the grand jury panel, despite their status as named suspects in earlier police affidavits.

By September 1999, the grand jury voted to indict John and Patsy Ramsey on two counts each of child abuse resulting in death and accessory to first-degree murder, finding probable cause that they had permitted the child to be placed in a situation where her life was endangered and had aided the perpetrator after the fact.
Handwriting analysis commissioned by the police showed similarities between the JonBenét Ramsey ransom note and the handwriting of Patsy Ramsey, though no definitive match was confirmed.

The 2016 CBS documentary 'The Case Of: JonBenét Ramsey' popularized a theory that nine-year-old Burke Ramsey struck JonBenét Ramsey with a flashlight during a sibling dispute over a snack, causing a fatal head injury, and that the parents subsequently staged a garrote strangulation and ransom note to cover up the incident.

Investigators in the 2016 CBS documentary 'The Case Of: JonBenét Ramsey', including former FBI profiler Bob Miller, contended that John and Patsy Ramsey obstructed the investigation to protect their son, Burke Ramsey.

In 1999, a Boulder grand jury of 12 members voted to indict John and Patsy Ramsey on charges of child abuse resulting in death and accessory to a crime, based on findings that they permitted JonBenét Ramsey to be injured by another and hindered law enforcement's apprehension of the responsible party.
District Attorney Alex Hunter declined to issue the indictments against John and Patsy Ramsey in 1999, citing insufficient probable cause for conviction.
The Boulder Police Department received a 911 call from Patsy Ramsey at 5:52 a.m. regarding the kidnapping of JonBenét Ramsey.
FBI agent John Walker identified the failure to promptly interview Patsy and John Ramsey as the investigation's gravest oversight, noting it hindered early suspect development.

Boulder County District Attorney Alex Hunter oversaw a 13-month grand jury probe starting in 1998, which voted on October 13, 1999, to indict John and Patsy Ramsey on charges of child abuse resulting in death and accessory after the fact to first-degree murder.

Alex Hunter declined to sign the indictment against John and Patsy Ramsey, stating that insufficient evidence existed for a conviction beyond a reasonable doubt, despite the grand jury finding probable cause.
In 2008, Boulder District Attorney Mary Lacy publicly exonerated John, Patsy, and Burke Ramsey, relying on touch DNA from an unknown male found on JonBenét Ramsey's long johns and clothing.

John and Patsy Ramsey initiated a defamation lawsuit in October 1999 against former Boulder Police Department detective Steve Thomas, seeking $80 million in damages over statements in Thomas's resignation letter and book that accused the parents of the murder.
The defamation lawsuit filed by John and Patsy Ramsey against Steve Thomas was settled out of court in March 2002 for an undisclosed sum, with Thomas agreeing not to repeat the specific allegations.

Type 1 allostatic overload is characterized by decreased thyroid hormone (T3) levels as the body conserves energy and can resolve once the perturbation passes and resources become available.
Type 2 allostatic overload is characterized by elevated thyroid hormone (T3) levels and can only be counteracted through learning, behavioral change, or social transformation.

Type 1 allostatic overload activates an 'emergency life history stage' characterized by a shift to survival mode, escape responses, and decreased thyroid hormone (T3) levels to conserve energy; it can resolve once the perturbation passes and resources become available.

Type 2 allostatic overload involves chronic elevation of stress mediators without escape response activation, is characterized by elevated thyroid hormone (T3) levels, and is associated with social conflict, dysfunction, and modern chronic stress situations.
Hypothyroidism (low levels of thyroid hormone) is the most common thyroid problem resulting from cancer treatment.

Information cascades, a phenomenon where groups are prone to epistemic problems when combining beliefs, were first identified by Bikhchandani et al. in 1992.

Information cascades occur when individuals update their beliefs based on the observed actions of others rather than their own private information, leading to a lack of independence in decision-making.

In an information cascade, a group of agents may collectively act against their private information, such as a group where almost all agents have private information that Nissan stock is better than GM stock, but the first agent buys GM stock based on their minority private information.

Zhu, L., Li, H., Wang, F. K., He, W., and Tian, Z. (2020) authored the paper 'How online reviews affect purchase intention: a new model based on the stimulus-organism-response (S-O-R) framework,' published in the Aslib Journal of Information Management, volume 72, pages 463–488.
The Stimulus–Organism–Response (SOR) model, which emphasizes the 'organism' component, remains a central focus of contemporary consumer behavior research.

Affective computing is becoming increasingly important in marketing, with attempts being made to track the 'O' (organism) component of Stimulus-Organism-Response (SOR) models via automatic emotion recognition or psychophysiological measurement parameters.
Rana et al. (2023) applied the Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model to assess how reviewers' ratings influence online purchase motives.

In e-commerce, external stimuli (S) within the Stimulus-Organism-Response model include website interfaces, product arrangements, navigation ease, and promotional cues such as limited-time discounts or offers.

The Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model, grounded in environmental psychology, posits that external stimuli (S) such as website visuals and interactions impact a consumer's internal state (O) of emotions and thoughts, which subsequently leads to a behavioral response (R) of making an impulsive purchase.

The Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) framework, Dual-Process Theory, and the Theory of Planned Behavior are theoretical models that explain the interaction between environmental stimuli and internal psychological processes in the context of impulse buying.
The Stimulus Organism Response (SOR) model is used by Hashmi, Attiq, and Rasheed (2019) to analyze factors affecting online impulsive buying behavior.
The Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model posits that environmental stimuli impact an individual's cognitive and emotional reactions, which subsequently result in behavior.
The Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model demonstrates that environmental stimuli influence internal cognitive or affective states, which motivate individuals to respond with a particular action.

The paper 'Factors Affecting Online Impulsive Buying Behavior: A Stimulus Organism Response Model Approach' by Hashmi, Attiq, and Rasheed utilizes the Stimulus-Organism-Response (SOR) model to analyze factors influencing impulsive buying in online environments, published in Market Forces in 2019.
Karim et al. (2021) analyzed factors influencing impulse buying behavior on e-tailing sites using the Stimulus-Organism-Response (SOR) model in Contemporary Management Research.

The Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) framework is a model used to structure variables to test how external stimuli impact organisms, leading to consumer responses, and has been applied to assess online consumer behavior in contexts such as social commerce, e-commerce, e-tailing sites, and live streaming.

The Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) framework, Dual-Process Theory, and the Theory of Planned Behavior are theoretical models that explain the interaction between environmental stimuli and internal psychological processes in impulsive buying.
