Immanuel Kant denied that introspection presents humans with any sort of self-as-object, echoing an insight previously held by David Hume.

David Hume claimed that causal relations are unintelligible and mysterious because humans do not experience the power, energy, or other qualities that are ordinarily taken to characterize causal relations.

David Hume argues that when he examines his own consciousness, he never encounters a 'self' but only stumbles upon particular perceptions such as heat, cold, light, shade, love, hatred, pain, or pleasure.

David Hume writes: 'For my part when I enter most intimately into what I call myself, I always stumble on some particular perception or other, of heat or cold, light or shade, love or hatred, pain or pleasure. I never can catch myself at any time without a perception, and never can observe anything but the perception.'
David Hume posits that the idea of the self is nothing more than a 'heap or collection of different perceptions' because there is no impression or perception of the owner of these perceptions.
Bertrand Russell initially favored the idea that humans are acquainted with themselves in his early work, but by the 1920s, he endorsed a view more aligned with David Hume's skeptical account.
David Hume rejected the idea that introspection reveals the self, claiming that when he introspected, he could never catch himself, but only perceptions.
Philosophers who support David Hume's view that introspection reveals only perceptions include Shoemaker (1986), Martin (1997), Howell (2010), and Prinz (2012).
Philosophers who oppose David Hume's view that introspection reveals only perceptions include Chisholm (1976), Cassam (1995), G. Strawson (2009), Damasio (2010), and Rosenthal (2012).

David Hume's skepticism about self-perception is challenged by the observation that while Hume claimed he could find no self in introspection, he reported his findings in first-personal terms, indicating he was aware of his mental states as his own, as noted by Roderick Chisholm (1976).
Terence Penelhum analyzed themes in the philosophy of David Hume in his 2000 book 'Themes in Hume'.
A.E. Pitson examined David Hume's philosophy of the self in his 2002 book 'Hume’s Philosophy of the Self'.
Galen Strawson analyzed David Hume's views on personal identity in the 2011 book 'The Evident Connexion: Hume on Personal Identity'.

Udo Thiel examined the history of self-consciousness and personal identity from René Descartes to David Hume in his 2011 book 'The Early Modern Subject: Self-Consciousness and Personal Identity from Descartes to Hume'.
David Hume proposed that the self is a 'bundle of perceptions' rather than an entity distinct from those perceptions.
David Hume argued that the connection between causes and effects cannot be explained or understood because humans only perceive causes followed by effects, rather than any binding powers or forces.

The mind-body problem became the central question of epistemology and modern philosophy due to the problematization of the mind and its relation to reality by René Descartes, John Locke, George Berkeley, David Hume, and Immanuel Kant.
George Berkeley, David Hume, and Immanuel Kant contested the possibility of acquiring reliable knowledge about the physical world.

David Hume suggested in 2017 that there is no self in the sense of Descartes's cogito, but only a flux of impressions and ideas, including memories of past impressions, which leads to the fiction of a self-identity.

von Stillfried (2018) argues that David Chalmers' definition of consciousness is circular because, if experience is assumed to be the only intrinsic evidence, the causal structure of time-space cannot be differentiated from cognitive structures, a problem previously demonstrated by David Hume and Immanuel Kant.
David Hume authored 'A Treatise of Human Nature', originally published in 1739, with a 2000 edition edited by D.F. Norton and M.J. Norton published by Oxford University Press.
David Hume argued that humans do not experience a necessary connection between cause and effect.
Hedda Hassel Mørch argues against David Hume that experience reveals a necessary connection between the mental events of feeling pain and trying to avoid pain, using this as a basis for panpsychism.

Uwe Meixner divides formulations of panpsychism into dualist and idealist versions, further dividing the latter into "atomistic idealistic panpsychism" (ascribed to David Hume) and "holistic idealistic panpsychism" (which Meixner favors).

Associationist psychology, as pursued by John Locke, David Hume (1739), and James Mill (1829), aimed to discover the principles by which conscious thoughts or ideas interact with or affect one another.
David Hume rejected the claim that the self is something over and above its contents, instead proclaiming the self to be nothing more than a concatenation of its ephemeral contents.
David Hume criticized the conception of substance for lacking empirical content, arguing that searching for the owner of properties that make up a substance reveals only further properties.
David Hume claimed the mind is nothing but a 'bundle' or 'heap' of impressions and ideas, which are particular mental states or events without an owner.
David Hume's bundle theory is a theory about the nature of the unity of the mind and is not necessarily dualist.
Derek Parfit (1970, 1984) and Sydney Shoemaker (1984) accept David Hume's bundle theory as physicalists.
Physicalists generally accept David Hume's bundle theory unless they wish to ascribe the unity of the mind to the brain or the organism as a whole.
David Hume held the view that subjects of mental states are merely bundles of immaterial states, rather than distinct entities over and above those states.

David Hume is credited with the "bundle" theory of the self, which asserts that mental states exist without an underlying subject or substance to possess them, as described in his work "Treatise Book I, Part IV, section VI."

David Hume asserted that when he introspects, he only encounters specific perceptions like heat, cold, light, shade, love, hatred, pain, or pleasure, and can never observe a self independent of these perceptions.

In the Appendix to his work, David Hume expressed dissatisfaction with his own bundle theory of the self because he could not reconcile two principles: that all distinct perceptions are distinct existences, and that the mind never perceives any real connection between distinct existences.

George Berkeley entertained a theory similar to David Hume's bundle theory in his Philosophical Commentaries (Notebook A, paragraphs 577-81), but later rejected it in favor of the claim that humans can have a notion, though not an idea, of the self.

David Hume believed that an impression might 'float free' from the mind to which it belonged, implying that the identity conditions of individual mental states are independent of the identity of the person who possesses them.

David Hume described the mind as a theatre, stating: "The mind is a kind of theatre where several perceptions successively make their appearance; pass, re-pass, glide away and mingle in an infinite variety of postures and situations."

David Hume's original philosophical position denies that humans have any sense of self, whereas versions of bundle theory that allow for awareness of relatedness accommodate the sense of self by explaining it as an illusion.
Criticism of arguments and intuitions regarding identity, spanning from David Hume to Derek Parfit (1970, 1984), has resulted in an inconclusive clash of intuitions.
David Hume, in the appendix to the Treatise, declared himself mystified by the problem of the unity of the mind and rejected his own initial solution.
While David Hume accepted the consequence that mental contents could exist alone, most philosophers regard the idea of a mind consisting of a lone pain or red after-image as absurd.
The philosophical problem of relating visual and auditory fields to produce a 'unity of apperception' has roots in the philosophy of David Hume.
The debate over whether William James's position on consciousness improves upon David Hume's or merely mystifies it remains unresolved.

René Descartes (1644) and John Searle (1992) posit that conscious experiences exist as modes or states of a conscious self or subject, rather than as isolated mental atoms, a view contrasted by David Hume (1739).

W. V. O. Quine's version of naturalized epistemology considers reasons for doubt about the fruitfulness of traditional philosophic study of scientific knowledge, citing the failure of philosophers to find satisfactory answers to radical skepticism and David Hume's criticism of induction.

David Hume questioned whether there is a valid justification for believing the conclusions of inductive arguments, noting that premises do not entail conclusions and that justifying induction often relies on induction itself.

David Hume argues that even if human perceptions are accurate, skepticism remains because humans perceive only a small part of the universe at any moment, yet claim knowledge of the world beyond current perceptions.
David Hume argues that reason is incapable of providing justification for any belief about the external world that extends beyond the scope of current sense perceptions.

David Hume argues that the argument for the persistence of the external world is fallacious because it equivocates between 'qualitative identity,' where two experiences share similar qualities, and 'numerical identity,' where one object is responsible for multiple experiences.
David Hume questions whether beliefs about the future and the unobserved, which rely on the assumption that the future will be like the past, are justified.
David Hume argues that the Principle of the Uniformity of Nature (PUN) relies on an unsupported premise, which makes it difficult to justify inductive arguments about the future and the unobserved.
In the context of empiricism, the term 'experience' refers to ordinary physical sensations, which David Hume referred to as 'impressions'.

Classical empiricists like John Locke and David Hume explained that complex concepts, such as dragons, are formed by reducing them to simple concepts derived from impressions, such as wings, the body of a snake, and the head of a horse.
The earliest clear statements of the reductionist and non-reductionist positions in the epistemology of testimony appear in the works of David Hume and Thomas Reid.

Historical antecedents to the contemporary literature on the epistemology of testimony include John Locke’s 'Essay Concerning Human Understanding', David Hume’s 'On Miracles', and Thomas Reid’s 'An Inquiry into the Human Mind' and 'Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man'.
Philosophers David Hume and Thomas Reid endorsed social views of knowledge, contrasting with the individualist motto of the British Royal Society.

David Hume argued that the trustworthiness of scholarship is established through expert scrutiny, while Thomas Reid argued for the principle of credulity, which asserts the right to trust the word of others.

David Hume stated: "[T]here is no species of reasoning more common, more useful, and more necessary to human life, than that which is derived from the testimony of men, and the reports of eye-witnesses and spectators. … [O]ur assurance in any argument of this kind is derived from no other principle than our observation of the veracity of human testimony, and of the usual conformity of facts to the reports of witnesses."

David Hume's reductionist perspective posits that individuals properly form beliefs based on testimony only because they have observed other confirmed instances of the veracity of human testimony, meaning testimonial justification is reducible to perceptual, memorial, and inferential justification.
David Hume does not include a priori insight among the sources to which testimonial justification is reduced.

The debate between David Hume and Thomas Reid regarding internal conditions on testimonially-based beliefs can be characterized by whether testimonially-based justification is reducible to other forms of justification.
The debate between David Hume and Thomas Reid regarding testimonially-based beliefs can be characterized by the extent to which such beliefs are implicitly inferential.
Coady documents that David Hume, when describing the inductive base for a belief in the reliability of testimony, mistakenly relies on evidence drawn from other people's testimony.
David Hume published 'An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding' in 1748.
Thomas Reid, a contemporary of David Hume, was a critic of testimonial reductionism, specifically arguing that it relies too heavily on individual observation.
In 'An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding', originally published in 1777, David Hume discusses the epistemology of testimony.

David Hume defines ideas as the “copies” of “impressions,” where impressions are “vivid” and “lively” as received directly from sense experience, while ideas are mental copies of inward or outward impressions that are “faint” or “feeble.”
David Hume argues that where there are no impressions, there can be no ideas, using the example that a blind man can have no notion of color.
David Hume asserts that humans are not born with innate ideas, though he agrees that tendencies to avoid pain or seek passions and desires are innate.

David Hume argues that the idea of God is a posteriori because no one has experienced God directly to form an impression, so the imagination forms the idea by extending experience of good qualities possessed by people.
David Hume asserts that reasoning aims to gain knowledge of the world through two methods: "relations of ideas" and "matters of fact."
David Hume argues that the method of deduction establishes relations between ideas that have already been acquired through experience, such as the definition of a mother as a woman parent.
David Hume divided human understanding into two categories: relations of ideas and matters of fact.
Relations of ideas, as defined by David Hume, concern matters like logic and mathematics, do not depend on how the world actually is, and are known a priori.
Matters of fact, as defined by David Hume, are derived from observation or generalization from experiences and are a posteriori.
John Locke, George Berkeley, and David Hume are the British philosophers most associated with establishing empiricism as a leading theory of knowledge.
David Hume asserts that the initiator of knowledge is experiential content, specifically impressions acquired through sensory activity.
David Hume argues that impressions are the direct causes of representations, rather than representations causing impressions.
John Locke and David Hume argue that empiricism provides a superior theory of knowledge by relying on a posteriori knowledge.
David Hume asserts that matters of fact are based on observation and experience, and are often generalizations arrived at through induction.
David Hume argued that knowledge of facts is never certain, while knowledge of relations between ideas, such as mathematical truths, can be certain but provides no information about the world.
The distinction between types of knowledge came to prominence in the 17th century and acted as a crucial factor in the philosophies of David Hume and Immanuel Kant.
David Hume argued for the trustworthiness of scholarship through expert scrutiny.
Few contemporary philosophers endorse the full form of David Hume's reductionist or inferentialist approach to testimonially-based belief.

C.A.J. Coady argues that David Hume's explanation for why testimonial beliefs are supported inductively is mistaken because Hume relies on evidence from other people to establish the inductive base for the reliability of testimony.

Reductionist views on testimony trace back to the work of David Hume (1740, 1748), with further developments by Traiger (1993, 2010), Faulkner (1998), Root (2001), Fogelin (2005), van Cleve (2006), Gelfert (2010), and Shieber (2015).
Paul Faulkner published a reductionist account of David Hume's epistemology of testimony in 1998.
Robert J. Fogelin published a defense of David Hume's views on miracles in 2005.
Axel Gelfert revisited David Hume's views on testimony in a 2010 article.

David Owen's 1987 article 'Hume versus Price on Miracles and Prior Probabilities: Testimony and the Bayesian Calculation' compares the views of Hume and Price on miracles, testimony, and Bayesian probability.
Michael Root analyzes David Hume's views on the virtues of testimony in his 2001 paper published in the American Philosophical Quarterly.
Jordan Howard Sobel provides a Bayesian interpretation of David Hume's analysis of the evidence of testimony for miracles in his 1987 paper published in The Philosophical Quarterly.
Saul Traiger published the article 'Humean Testimony' in the Pacific Philosophical Quarterly in 1993.
Saul Traiger published the article 'Experience and Testimony in Hume’s Philosophy' in the journal Episteme in 2010.
David Hume's problem of induction makes it difficult to define what constitutes the foundation in foundationalist epistemology.
David Hume proposed that ideas become beliefs when they are given force by passions, emotions, direct experience, or the imagination.
David Hume wrote: '... in matters of religion men take a pleasure in being terrify’d …'

family.name: Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware

facts.content: The authors of the article 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' collaborated for six years within European research projects on the topic of Open Design.

The authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' investigate how Open Design practices challenge available descriptive theories of design and established prescriptive design methods.

The article 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' addresses three essential questions: What is the definition of Open Design, how does Open Design work and differ from closed design, and when is Open Design a successful practice?

The authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' developed seven observations based on six years of research on Open Design and Open Source Hardware, which they intend to serve as an agenda for further research.

The authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' suggest that design processes where requirement management and product architecting are decentralized among a swarm of participants need to be experimented with at a larger scale to understand their relevance.

The authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' suggest investigating the role of product architecture in supporting Open Design strategies because it is independent of process and governance models and may apply across all types of Open Design initiatives.

The authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' define 'structure openness' as an aspect of openness that is often disregarded in design literature but has practical implications for Open Design.

The authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' define 'process openness' as the collective nature of processes that allow the participation of any interested person.

To explain the concept of process openness, the authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' utilize terms including 'community', 'participation', 'volunteer', 'external people', and 'core team'.
The authors of 'Seven observations and research questions about Open Design and Open Source Hardware' identify Open Design as an expression of postmodernity and a critique of progress.

The author identifies a frontier-tech consortium operating at the intersection of artificial intelligence, consciousness studies, computational biology, nuclear-grade compute, and neurotechnology, which supports machine learning, cognitive modeling, consciousness transfer, and life-extension systems.

The neurotechnology used by the frontier-tech consortium underwrites machine learning, cognitive modeling, and the early scaffolding for consciousness transfer and life-extension systems.

Bacillus subtilis spores used in biocomposite thermoplastic polyurethanes are engineered to withstand the high temperatures required for polyurethane production and include a fluorescent reporter to monitor spore germination.
Tang, T. C. et al. developed biocomposite thermoplastic polyurethanes that incorporate evolved bacterial spores as living fillers to facilitate the disintegration of the polymer.

Hao et al. (2025) proposed COCONUT, a method that allows models to reason in a continuous latent space by feeding hidden states back as subsequent inputs, enabling the encoding of multiple reasoning branches simultaneously.
Consuming foods high in saturated fat, such as coconut, dairy, and red meat, may raise bad cholesterol and negatively impact heart health, potentially worsening inflammation.
Low light conditions in plants result in reduced dry matter, photosynthetic rate, transpiration, stomatal conductance, and stem thickness, as reported by Jin et al. (2023) and Huang et al. (2024).
Wang et al. (2017) analyzed the physiological characteristics, dry matter, and active component accumulation patterns of Changium smyrnioides in response to a light intensity gradient.
Examples of the Ruler archetype in pop culture include Cersei Lannister from 'Game of Thrones', Miranda Priestly from 'The Devil Wears Prada', and King Arthur.
Examples of the Lover archetype in fiction include Romeo and Juliet, Jack and Rose from Titanic, and Jaime Lannister from Game of Thrones.
In modern television, protagonists in shows like Breaking Bad and Game of Thrones often begin as traditional heroes but evolve into anti-heroes, challenging conventional archetype boundaries.

The Hero archetype has evolved from figures like King Arthur or Odysseus to nuanced characters like Jon Snow from Game of Thrones or Tony Stark from Iron Man, who are plagued by doubt, flaws, and moral ambiguity, according to Monaghan and Combs (2014).

The rise of the 'dark feminine' in characters such as Cersei Lannister from Game of Thrones or Villanelle from Killing Eve represents a departure from traditional archetypal roles assigned to women, embodying the Shadow and Trickster archetypes, according to Gilligan (2010).

Tom Fletcher stated: “Civilians must be protected – full stop.”

Tom Fletcher warned that escalating violence in the Middle East risks diverting international attention from other humanitarian emergencies, including those in Sudan, South Sudan, Ukraine, and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
Tom Fletcher stated: “What we need is de-escalation, an immediate cessation of hostilities and genuine dialogue and negotiations.”

The study 'Circadian rhythms in bipolar disorder patient-derived neurons predict lithium response: preliminary studies' was published in Molecular Psychiatry in 2021 (DOI: 10.1038/s41380-021-01048-7).
Researchers studied treatment resistance in depression using patient-derived neurons in vitro, as detailed in a 2019 publication in Molecular Psychiatry.
Vadodaria et al. (2019) identified altered serotonergic circuitry in patient-derived neurons from individuals with SSRI-resistant major depressive disorder, as published in Molecular Psychiatry.

Vadodaria et al. published a study titled 'Serotonin-induced hyperactivity in SSRI-resistant major depressive disorder patient-derived neurons' in the journal Molecular Psychiatry in 2019, which demonstrates that neurons derived from patients with SSRI-resistant major depressive disorder exhibit hyperactivity when exposed to serotonin.
Vadodaria et al. (2017) utilized patient-derived neurons to model serotonin-related psychiatric conditions in vitro.

K.C. Vadodaria, D.N. Amatya, M.C. Marchetto, and F.H. Gage authored the paper 'Modeling psychiatric disorders using patient stem cell-derived neurons: a way forward,' published in Genome Medicine in 2018.

The global energy footprint, including energy-dedicated biomass feedstocks, is projected to grow by more than 2 million km2 by 2050 across decarbonization scenarios.

GSS 3304.01: Gender, Power, and Justice explores theoretical paradigms for social experiences, including theories of gendered, racial, economic, and sexual inequities, discipline, ideology, separatism, coalition, community, vulnerability, interdependence, oppression, and resistance.

During the first lockdown, 58% of executives and middle-ranking positions in France worked from home, compared to 20% of non-managerial roles and 2% of manual workers, according to the French national statistical office, INSEE.

The INSEE focus report 'Conditions de vie pendant le confinement : des écarts selon le niveau de vie et la catégorie socioprofessionnelle' (no 197), published on June 19, 2020, analyzes living conditions during the lockdown, highlighting disparities based on standard of living and socio-professional category.

Children who are over-scheduled and experience constant parental pressure to excel may suffer from increased anxiety, lower self-esteem, and reduced autonomy, according to a 2011 study by LeMoyne and Buchanan.

To mitigate the negative effects of over-scheduling, parents should allow children to choose extracurricular activities that are intrinsically motivating and ensure a balance that includes time for Free Play.

Theoretical studies of interacting spins have demonstrated that entangled states can be maintained in noisy open quantum systems at high temperatures and far from thermal equilibrium through a 'recoherence' mechanism.

Theoretical studies of interacting spins demonstrate that entangled states can be maintained in noisy open quantum systems at high temperatures and far from thermal equilibrium by using a 'recoherence' mechanism to counterbalance decoherence, suggesting entanglement may persist in environments like the brain.

A significant portion of the annual tax gap in the United States results from professionals, such as physicians and attorneys, failing to report self-employment income and from wealthy individuals and families claiming false deductions.

Reducing tax expenditures that benefit wealthy families while expanding tax credits and deductions focused on caregiving, family functioning, and labor force participation could help equalize economic outcomes for families.
Implementing year-round tax strategies, including the use of available deductions and credits, enhances savings and supports comprehensive financial planning.

Psychologist Carl Jung might have viewed the reptilian alien narrative as a modern manifestation of ancient archetypes, specifically the shadow and the trickster, expressed through contemporary technological imagery.

According to Wikipedia, Jungian character archetypes refer to "unclear underlying forms or the archetypes-as-such from which emerge images and motifs such as the mother, the child, the trickster, and the flood among others. History, culture and personal context shape these manifest representations thereby giving them their specific content."
Archetypes such as the Hero, Shadow, Trickster, and Caregiver manifest in contemporary films, television, video games, advertising, and social media.

The Trickster archetype is characterized by its disruptive, playful, and often morally ambiguous nature, acting as a catalyst for change and transformation by defying conventional rules and boundaries.
Carl Jung (1959) stated that the Trickster archetype embodies unconscious forces that challenge the ego and promote psychological growth through disruption and chaos.

Loki from the Marvel Cinematic Universe and the Joker from The Dark Knight trilogy are examples of the Trickster archetype, which uses deception and unpredictability to challenge established order and provoke reflection on societal norms.

Homer Simpson from the television series The Simpsons reflects the Trickster archetype through his disregard for societal rules and chaotic behavior, which questions norms and disrupts the status quo.
Characters in the television series Rick and Morty utilize the Trickster archetype to undermine traditional moral frameworks and expose the absurdity of human existence.
The Trickster archetype often appears when a system or psyche is rigid and requires breakdown to facilitate new growth.
The Trickster archetype is a prominent figure in video games and interactive media, characterized by cunning, mischief, and the ability to subvert established rules.
Video games such as Portal and Grand Theft Auto V feature characters who embody the Trickster archetype, which challenges societal norms and provides players with a sense of rebellious freedom.
The Trickster archetype introduces surprise and chaos into gameplay, which forces players to adapt to unexpected situations and think creatively, according to Jung (1959).
Traditional archetypes such as the Hero, Trickster, and Shadow have evolved in modern media in response to contemporary cultural values and societal complexities.
Mary, a hypothetical subject who knows all physical facts about colors, learns a new phenomenal fact upon seeing a red rose for the first time, which suggests that phenomenal facts are non-physical.

The knowledge argument, or Mary's Room, is a thought experiment involving a neuroscientist named Mary who has lived in a black-and-white room and knows everything about the brain and color perception, but has never seen color.

Frank Jackson's hypothetical scenario of Mary, the super color scientist, illustrates the claim that third-person theory has limits, specifically regarding what can be understood about experiencing red without having had that experience.

In a Gettier case adapted from Zagzebski (1996), Mary enters her house and sees a familiar appearance in her husband's chair, leading her to believe her husband is sitting in the living room. However, she has misidentified the man, who is actually her husband's brother, while her husband is actually dozing in a different chair out of her sight.
A theological objection to the idea of an alien Incarnation is the requirement for a figure like Mary to be uniquely chosen for each alien species.
Mary was spared from sin and uniquely chosen by God for her role in salvation history.
The author posits that God requires an invitation, similar to Mary's 'yes', to enter a world through an Incarnation.

The ENGL 3658W Latino-American Literature course at Vanderbilt University examines literature by Chicano, Puerto Rican, Cuban, Dominican, and Latin American writers in the United States, focusing on constructs of Latino identity including race, class, gender, and the basis for immigration in the context of American culture.

The musculoskeletal system includes muscles, all bones of the skeleton, and connective tissues such as ligaments and tendons.
The musculoskeletal system is composed of bones, muscles, and connective tissues, and functions as the body's framework for support, protection, and movement.
Connective tissues, such as ligaments and tendons, link bones to each other and secure muscles to bones.
The dermis is a layer of connective tissue located beneath the epidermis that contains blood vessels and nerves supplying the skin.
The hypodermis, also called the underlying fascia, consists of fat, connective tissue, and skin appendages such as hair, nails, and sebaceous and sweat glands.
The labia majora contain loose connective and adipose tissue intermixed with smooth muscle, which resembles the dartos muscle of the scrotum.
The hymen is connective tissue that covers the vaginal opening.
The cardinal ligament consists of connective tissue strands associated with uterine and vaginal vessels located within the broad base of the broad ligament, which help support the uterus and vagina.
The ovarian cortex contains follicles at various stages of development and degeneration and is composed of tightly packed connective tissue.
Connective tissues bind together various body structures and are composed of widely spaced cells and large amounts of intercellular matrix.
Bone and blood are specialized connective tissues in the human body, characterized by hard and liquid intercellular matrices, respectively.
The skeletal system is composed of bones, joints, and connective tissues.
Tendons are tough, fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones and transmit the force generated by muscle contraction to the bones to enable movement.
The human body contains approximately 200 different types of cells, which are categorized into four basic classes: epithelial tissues, muscle tissues, nerve tissues, and connective tissues.

The stomach is an organ composed of muscle, connective, epithelial, and nervous tissues, where muscle and connective tissues form the wall, epithelial and connective tissues form the lining, and nervous tissue extends throughout both the wall and the lining.
Ligaments are strong bands of connective tissue that connect bones to other bones within joints.
Tendons are tough bands of fibrous connective tissue that connect muscles to bones.
Fascia is a dense, fibrous connective tissue that surrounds and separates muscles, providing support and protection.

The four basic cell types in the human body, along with their extracellular materials, form epithelial tissues (covering body surfaces and lining internal organs), muscle tissues (capable of contraction), nerve tissues (conducting electrical impulses), and connective tissues (binding body structures together).
Estrogen increases collagen in connective tissues and loosens tendons and ligaments, making women naturally more flexible than men.
The proliferation phase of healing involves the activation of fibroblasts, which produce collagen to lay down new connective tissue to replace damaged or destroyed tissue.
Collagen is a primary protein found in human skin and connective tissues that facilitates the production of new connective tissue known as granulation tissue.

Nonspecific proliferative chronic inflammation is characterized by the presence of non-specific granulation tissue formed by the infiltration of mononuclear cells, such as lymphocytes, macrophages, and plasma cells, alongside the proliferation of fibroblasts, connective tissue, vessels, and epithelial cells, as seen in nasal or cervical polyps and lung abscesses.

Components of personal finance include checking and savings accounts, credit cards, consumer loans, stock market investments, retirement plans, social security benefits, insurance policies, and income tax management.

Building an inventory of assets, debts, and important financial information such as the location of a Will, power of attorney, insurance policies, and digital assets is a beneficial first step in estate planning.

To protect financial assets, individuals should create an emergency fund, define what constitutes an emergency with their partner, and purchase insurance policies such as homeowners, renters, health, car, disability, and life insurance.

Risk management in personal finance planning enables individuals to utilize insurance policies, generate more retirement savings, cushion against unexpected healthcare expenses, and build inheritance for the next generation.
Financial planners should verify that clients possess the correct insurance policies and adequate coverage levels rather than simply confirming the existence of a policy.

The process of managing insurance in financial planning involves five steps: (1) recognize the importance of life insurance for financial security, (2) determine the right coverage level based on your financial situation, (3) seek personalized guidance from a financial advisor to find suitable options, (4) periodically review and adjust insurance policies to match life changes, and (5) confirm that insurance choices align with your financial objectives.

Individuals should follow these steps when a major life event occurs: (1) Assess the impact of the event on financial goals, (2) Update insurance policies to align with new family responsibilities, (3) Reallocate investments based on changing income levels and expenses, (4) Consider consulting a financial planner for tailored advice, and (5) Regularly review financial plans to stay aligned with personal circumstances.
Insurance policies should be individualized to a person's specific situation, taking into account their estate plan, legacy, and wishes.
The performance of insurance policies often fluctuates over time due to factors such as interest rate changes.
When assessing insurance policies, individuals should evaluate optimal ownership structures, beneficiary designations, exposure to negative tax treatment, and policy competitiveness.

Conducting an annual financial plan review that includes a thorough analysis of existing insurance policies can help identify more attractively priced policies, stronger guarantees, and additional beneficial policy attributes.
Significant life events, such as getting married or starting a business, may necessitate revisions to an individual's insurance policy.

Critical personal financial records that should be stored securely include agreements with personal finance advisors, financial plan statements, investment mixes, periodic review reports, certificates, account statements, and insurance policies.
Self-insuring involves setting aside money to cover potential losses instead of purchasing an insurance policy.
Paying out of pocket for losses can be more cost-effective than purchasing an insurance policy in certain situations.
Before purchasing insurance, individuals should evaluate the likelihood of a loss, the potential financial impact of that loss, and the cost of the insurance policy.
Insurance policies transfer the risk of loss from an individual to an insurance company, where the insurance company agrees to pay for covered losses up to policy limits in exchange for a premium.

Jacob Kujala, a senior product manager for U.S. Bancorp Advisors, stated: "Your insurance policies are unique and very individualized to your situation. Your estate plan, your legacy and your wishes after you’re gone must be taken into consideration."
Insurance policies can cover unavoidable critical costs, such as health care expenses, allowing individuals to incorporate emergency preparation into their overall financial strategy.

Carl and Linda Sagan intended for the human figures on the Pioneer Plaque to be more pan-racial, but technical limitations regarding the use of outlines rather than shades made this impossible.
The Voyager Golden Record was prepared by a committee of scientists and technicians chaired by Carl Sagan, who spent almost one year selecting the content.
Carl Sagan utilized a non-ethnocentric approach to select the musical content for the Voyager Golden Record, consulting historians, anthropologists, and musicologists.

Between 1962 and 1995, the community transmitting messages into space consisted of a closed circle of policymakers and a small scientific elite, with three of the six messages transmitted during this period involving central figures Carl Sagan and Frank Drake.

Carl Sagan stated: "We find that we live on an insignificant planet of a humdrum star lost in a galaxy tucked away in some forgotten corner of a universe in which there are far more galaxies than people."
Carl Sagan (1934–1996), an American astronomer and agnostic, stated: 'science has systematically expropriated areas which are the traditional concern of religion.'
Carl Sagan denied being an atheist, as reported by Joel Achenbach in The Washington Post on July 10, 2014.
Carl Sagan discussed extraterrestrial hypotheses in his contribution to the book 'UFO’s — A Scientific Debate' (1972).
Carl Sagan and Paul Horowitz published data from a 5-year search and 2-year follow-up for extraterrestrial signals in The Astrophysical Journal in 1993.
Carl Sagan and Paul Horowitz identified 11 'events' after three sky surveys that satisfied all criteria for being acknowledged as genuinely alien signals except for verifiability.
The Betty and Barney Hill abduction case inspired an episode of 'The X-Files', the NBC series 'Dark Skies', and was discussed by Carl Sagan on his show 'Cosmos'.
Carl Sagan discussed the Betty and Barney Hill abduction case on his television show "Cosmos."
Carl Sagan stated that the alien abduction experience is similar to tales of demon abduction common throughout history.
The Betty and Barney Hill abduction case inspired an episode of "The X-Files," the NBC series "Dark Skies," and was discussed by Carl Sagan on his television show "Cosmos."

In his 1979 book 'Broca's Brain', astronomer Carl Sagan argued that because the Dogon appear to lack knowledge of planets beyond Saturn, their astronomical knowledge is more likely to have originated from European sources rather than extraterrestrial ones.
Carl Sagan's book 'Broca's Brain: Reflections on the Romance of Science' was published in 1979 and reprinted in 1986.

Carl Sagan and other scientists demonstrated that the existence of Sirius B could be inferred from the wobble of Sirius A using 19th-century telescopes, refuting the need for extraterrestrial explanation.

Neuropsychologist Richard Young stated regarding the triune brain model: "This was in the 1960s. And the triune brain captured the imagination of the intellectual class. Even Carl Sagan commented on it."

Carl Sagan stated in 'The Demon-Haunted World': 'There is no spaceship in these stories. But most of the central elements of the alien abduction account are present, including sexually obsessive non-humans who live in the sky, walk through walls, communicate telepathically, and perform breeding experiments on the human species. Unless we believe that demons really exist, how can we understand so strange a belief system, embraced by the whole Western world ... reinforced by personal experience in every generation, and taught by Church and State? Is there any real alternative besides a shared delusion based on common brain wiring and chemistry?'
Carl Sagan famously referred to the planet Earth as a 'pale blue dot'.
Carl Sagan and other scientists believe that because there are billions of stars in the galaxy, intelligent life must have evolved on at least a few of those stars.
Astronomer Carl Sagan wrote in his 1996 book 'The Demon-Haunted World: Science as a Candle in the Dark' that 'Pseudoscience speaks to powerful emotional needs that science often leaves unfulfilled.'

Carl Sagan's 1996 book, 'The Demon-Haunted World: Science as a Candle in the Dark', devotes two chapters to criticizing alien abductions, channellers, astrology, past life regression, and recovered memories of satanic ritual cults as pseudoscience.

During the 1970s and 1980s, prominent figures associated with the Committee for the Scientific Investigation of Claims of the Paranormal (CSICOP), including aviation journalist Philip J. Klass, illusionist James Randi, and astronomer Carl Sagan, acted as public myth-busters regarding paranormal claims.
In 1996, the PBS television program 'Nova' aired an episode about extraterrestrial life that featured a debate between John E. Mack and skeptics, including Carl Sagan.
Carl Sagan stated: “Many of the principal advocates of UFO abduction seem to want the validation of science without submitting to its rigorous standards of evidence.”

Carl Sagan stated: “Someone says something happened to them … And, people can say anything. The fact that someone says something doesn’t mean it’s true. Doesn’t mean they’re lying, but it doesn’t mean it’s true.”
Carl Sagan stated in his book 'The Demon-Haunted World' that the alien abduction experience is similar to historical tales of demon abduction.
Carl Sagan questioned whether there is any real alternative to understanding the alien abduction belief system besides it being a shared delusion based on common brain wiring and chemistry.
Cormac Colleran and Dr. Jodie Solberg are international hypnotherapists, trainers, and co-founders of the SFTI Hypnosis Academy.

Jodie and Cormac are the co-founders of SFTI Hypnosis Academy, an organization that trains practitioners worldwide in solution-focused hypnotherapy for trauma, addiction, anxiety, and unwanted patterns.

facts.content: To be included in the meta-analysis, studies had to meet four criteria: (1) patients were aged 18 to 65 and met DSM-III-R, DSM-IV, or DSM-5 diagnostic criteria for specific anxiety-related disorders; (2) patients were randomly assigned to either CBT or a placebo (pill or psychological); (3) anxiety symptom severity was assessed via a validated clinical interview or self-report instrument pre- and post-treatment; and (4) studies provided sufficient data to calculate effect sizes.
According to the DSM-III-R and DSM-IV, a PTSD diagnosis requires the existence of a recognizable trauma that would cause distress symptoms in most people.
Wakefield (1992) provided a conceptual critique of the DSM-III-R definition of mental disorder, arguing for a definition based on harmful dysfunction.
The influence of news media on voting behavior is a recurring topic on the research agenda, as noted by researchers such as Walgrave et al. (2008b) and Hooghe et al. (2011).
Wolfe and colleagues (2013) argue that studies on policy and decision-making should simultaneously account for the public agenda, the news media, and the policy agenda.
The news media shapes both the input (the salience and definition of issues) and the output (communicating policy decisions to the general public) of the political system.
Quantifying criteria for source selection and computing the cost of integrating a source helps save effort while producing a high-quality knowledge graph.

A Phase II multicenter trial led by Mark Zafereo and Maria Cabanillas increased average progression-free survival for patients with BRAF V600E-mutated anaplastic thyroid cancer from 6.7 months to over one year.
The Phase II trial for BRAF V600E-mutated anaplastic thyroid cancer evaluated a pre-surgical combination of the targeted therapies dabrafenib and trametinib plus the immunotherapy drug pembrolizumab.

The triple therapy regimen of dabrafenib, trametinib, and pembrolizumab enabled 74% of patients with BRAF V600E-mutated anaplastic thyroid cancer to have their tumor removed surgically, compared to a historical rate of 5%.
Dietary interventions through nutrition therapy are considered important for managing non-communicable diseases like obesity and type 2 diabetes and are supported by organizations worldwide.

Nutrition therapy for managing diabetes requires both reducing the consumption of unhealthy and ultra-processed foods and increasing dietary diversity to improve overall nutrient intake and health outcomes.
Evert et al. (2014) published nutrition therapy recommendations for the management of adults with diabetes in the journal Diabetes Care.
Evert et al. (2019) published a consensus report in Diabetes Care regarding nutrition therapy for adults with diabetes or prediabetes.
Sievenpiper et al. (2018) published a paper on nutrition therapy in the Canadian Journal of Diabetes.
The study analyzed imaging derived phenotypes (IDPs) of cortical and subcortical grey matter volume estimates and white matter hyperintensity (WMH) volume.
White matter hyperintensity (WMH) volume was log-transformed prior to analysis to account for the skewed nature of the data distribution.

The researchers tested differences in mean brain area volume using a permuted t-statistic with a Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons, using a corrected p-value of 0.05/140 to account for 139 brain regions and white matter hyperintensity volume.
Sleep duration is associated with white matter hyperintensity volume in older adults, as reported in the 2014 Northern Manhattan Study published in the Journal of Sleep Research.
Ramos et al. found that sleep duration is associated with white matter hyperintensity volume in older adults in the Northern Manhattan Study.

Integrated Information Theory postulates suggest that the neural correlate of being conscious is located in a temporal-parietal-occipital 'hot zone' of the brain, which possesses the neural architecture for reciprocal projections capable of manifesting a maximally irreducible cause-effect structure.
Integrated Information Theory (IIT) suggests that a complex of maximum phi resides primarily in the posterior cerebral cortex, specifically in a temporo–parietal–occipital ‘hot zone’.

Hemming (1949) identified the aims of social education as fostering: the development of spontaneity, self-reliance, flexibility of mind, clear thinking, tolerance, initiative, articulateness, adventurousness of outlook, courage in the face of new problems, enjoyment of created activity, sound standards of action and appreciation, world-mindedness, and a sense of purpose and philosophy of life.

Psychiatrist Dr. Alex Dimitriu states that anger suppresses the prefrontal cortex, the part of the brain responsible for clear thinking and self-control, which makes effective communication more difficult.

Some individuals are born with missing or irregularly-shaped reproductive organs, which can affect the functions of the reproductive system and lead to symptoms such as irregular bleeding, difficulty conceiving, or painful sex.
The pelvic cavity contains the end of the large intestine, rectum, urinary bladder, and internal reproductive organs.

The abdominopelvic cavity is not physically divided by a membrane, but is conceptually distinguished into the abdominal cavity, which houses digestive organs, and the pelvic cavity, which houses organs of reproduction.

Research indicates that there are far more similarities between female and male brains than there are differences, and brains are more comparable to organs like the heart and kidney than to reproductive organs.
The male torso is shorter compared to the female torso, which is longer to accommodate reproductive organs.
Missing or irregularly shaped reproductive organs can affect the function of the reproductive system, potentially leading to irregular bleeding, difficulty conceiving, or painful sex.

Human reproductive organs exhibit anatomical variation between individuals.
The size of reproductive organs in fresh-caught crabs varies according to the phase of the Moon.

The project team will use high-density Neuropixels probes to collect high-resolution data from a large part of the cortex across the entire anterior-posterior extent of the brain, and will employ optogenetics to causally manipulate brain networks to resolve key and opposing predictions of Global Neuronal Workspace and Integrated Information Theory.

The experimental protocol for non-human primates and mice involves recording neuronal activity using Neuropixels probes in multiple areas of the cortex while subjects perform a go/no-go discrimination task involving supra-threshold stimuli presented one at a time for a long duration, with task-irrelevant stimuli interleaved to minimize behavioral report confounds.
Neuropixels probes are used to record spiking activity and LFPs simultaneously from two PFC areas, two sensory areas, and one parietal area in NHPs and mice during behavioral tasks.
The feasibility of simultaneous recordings with Neuropixels probes has been demonstrated in both mice and NHPs.

The researchers will semi-chronically implant up to five NHP-version Neuropixels probes in non-human primates using customized 3D-printed holders affixed to the skull, utilizing 10 mm probes for frontal sites and 45 mm probes for posterior sites.

In non-human primates, the researchers will replace Neuropixels probes in the prefrontal cortex (areas 45/46) with linear electrode arrays (e.g., 24-contacts, 200 µm spacing) for stimulation across cortical layers after completing simultaneous recordings from PFC and posterior cortical sites.
van Daal RJJ et al. published a protocol in Nature Protocols in 2021 for the implantation of Neuropixels probes to perform chronic recording of neuronal activity in freely behaving mice and rats.
Takasugi et al. (2017) demonstrated that small extracellular vesicles secreted from senescent cells promote cancer cell proliferation through the EphA2 receptor.

The Riedel–Hiermaier–Thoma (RHT) constitutive model is used to design ultra-high-performance concrete.

Akhnoukh and Buckhalter (2021) reviewed the constituents, mechanical properties, applications, and current challenges of ultra-high-performance concrete, published in Case Studies in Construction Materials.
N. Wu et al. investigated the use of sugar cane bagasse ash as a cement replacement in ultra-high performance concrete (UHPC) in 2022.

Priority debts are defined as debts with the most serious consequences for non-payment, such as the loss of essential services like domestic utilities or court action that could lead to the loss of liberty, and these must be paid back before all other debts.
Vulnerable groups are more likely to have arrears on priority debts, such as domestic bills, which carry the most severe legal consequences.
Among respondents with mental health problems in the 2000 UK government survey, the most common arrears were priority debts, including domestic bills, rent, and local authority taxes.
Priority debts are defined as debts that can lead to immediate harm if left unpaid, such as eviction or repossession, and include mortgage, rent, utilities, car payments, taxes, and child support.

In the Goba District study, Physalis peruviana Mill, Rubus apetalus Poir, and Discorea bulbifera L were identified as the least preferred wild edible plants.

In a pair-wise ranking of wild edible plants in the Goba District, Physalis peruviana Mill and Rubus apetalus Poir were the least preferred species, with scores of 20 (9.6%) and 19 (9.1%) respectively.

Physalis peruviana Mill, Rubus apetalus Poir, and Discorea bulbifera L were identified as comparatively less preferred wild edible plants in the Goba District study, suggesting a lower degree of utilization or desirability compared to more preferred species.
Inflationary pressures caused by tariffs may be moderated over time if consumers shift consumption to domestically produced alternatives, provided those alternatives have sufficient supply capacity.
When tariffs increase the price of imports, consumers may substitute those goods with domestically produced alternatives or imports from countries not subject to the tariffs.

Humans are considered 'generalist specialists' because they occupy and utilize a diversity of environments while also specializing in adaptations to specific environmental extremes, as noted by Roberts and Stewart (2018).

Roberts et al. (2017) analyzed human stable isotope ecology and rainforest adaptations in Sri Lanka during the Late Pleistocene and Holocene periods, specifically between approximately 36,000 and 3,000 years ago.
Roberts et al. (2020) provided isotopic evidence indicating initial coastal colonization and subsequent diversification in the human occupation of Wallacea.

Roberts and West contend that research on cognitive biases suggests humans are best understood as manifesting natural epistemic defects, and that the goal of a cognizer should be to cultivate methods for avoiding or overcoming these defects through self-vigilance and intellectual vitality.

Roberts and Wood (2007) characterize intellectual courage and caution as virtues that dispose individuals to respond appropriately to intellectual threats, with intellectual courage functioning analogously to Aristotelian moral courage by balancing rashness and fear.

Intellectual virtues that have received less philosophical attention include intellectual generosity (Roberts & Wood 2007), epistemic temperance (Battaly 2010), open-mindedness (Adler 2004; Baehr 2011; Carter & Gordon 2014b), intellectual perseverance (King 2014b), inquisitiveness (L. Watson 2015), and curiosity (Alfano 2013a; Whitcomb 2010).

Philosophers including Axtell & Carter (2008), McDowell (1994), Roberts & Wood (2007), and Zagzebski (1996, 2009) argue that epistemological terms like 'knowledge', 'evidence', 'justification', 'duty', and 'virtue' cannot be adequately defined or fully explained in purely non-normative vocabulary.
Roberts and Wood (2007) argue that epistemology should inspire individuals with portraits of intellectual virtues to promote cultural reformation and intellectual flourishing.

Robert Roberts and W. Jay Wood (2007) define intellectual humility as a striking or unusual unconcern for social importance, characterizing it as a kind of emotional insensitivity to issues of status.

Robert Roberts and Jay Wood (2007) argue that conventional questions and methods have eviscerated epistemology and propose that the field should instead aim to reform intellectual culture by sketching nuanced pictures of intellectual virtues using literature, history, and scripture.
Unlike Allan Hazlett, Robert Roberts and Jay Wood emphasize the concerns and emotions of an intellectually humble person rather than their doxastic states.
ArcadeDB uses the query 'MATCH (p:Product)-[:SOURCED_FROM*1..5]->(s:Supplier)' to perform multi-tier provenance tracking via native graph traversal.
Current knowledge graph construction solutions have limited metadata support, with few approaches acknowledging the importance of provenance tracking and debugging capabilities.
Divided attention influences the human ability to perform multiple tasks simultaneously.
Men tend to excel at task-focused projects, while women are more likely to excel at language and multitasking.

The higher ratio of white matter in women may explain their proficiency in multi-tasking, social logic, and memory of words and faces, while the higher ratio of grey matter in men may explain their stronger connection to spatial and conceptual activities and gross motor skills.
Parents should support adolescent cognitive health by discouraging multitasking and minimizing notifications and distractions during study and learning time.

Chia-chen Yang, PhD, Associate Professor of Educational Psychology at Oklahoma State University, advises that teens should be encouraged to practice self-awareness by asking themselves how their phone use and multitasking have impacted their relationships with friends and others.

Martin Paulus, Scientific Director and President of the Laureate Institute for Brain Research, states that when individuals engage in cognitively effortful tasks like reading or listening, they cannot truly multitask; instead, the brain switches attention between tasks, which consumes processing power.
Martin Paulus asserts that building deep attention and focus on a single task for an extended period cannot occur in a multitasking environment, a principle that applies to both adults and children.

Susanne Baumgartner, PhD, Associate Professor at the Amsterdam School of Communication Research, advises parents to provide children with opportunities to avoid multitasking and instead focus on single tasks to build depth of experience.

Abe Flanigan, PhD, Associate Professor at Georgia Southern University, states that what is commonly labeled as 'multitasking' is actually 'task-switching,' where individuals rapidly alternate attention between different activities, such as social media and classroom instruction.

To overcome noise and distraction barriers, speakers should avoid multitasking (such as checking email or phones) during one-on-one meetings and ensure discussions occur in settings conducive to listening.

Open source projects such as Apache, Debian, Gnome, Mozilla, and Netbeans utilize a decentralized design for code acceptance, where committers or developers within a specific module are responsible for making decisions regarding code inclusion.

Several open source projects utilize democratic processes for leadership selection: Debian elects its Project Leader annually, FreeBSD committers choose their 'core team', and Netbeans developers choose their governing board.

Corporate open source networks are a form of tighter coupling where software corporations open specific technologies to hackers under OSS licensing terms by hosting a production platform on the Internet, with Mozilla and Netbeans serving as examples.

B.R. Giri analyzed the Haliya and Kamaiya bonded labor contracts in Nepal, specifically assessing the perspectives of the Musahar and Tharu communities.
The article 'The Bonded Labour System in Nepal: Perspectives of Haliya and Kamaiya Child Workers' was published in the Journal of Asian and African Studies, volume 44, issue 6, pages 599–623.

Anne Haas Dyson is a former teacher of young children and currently serves as a Professor of Education at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.
Adena B. Meyers holds a doctorate in clinical community psychology from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign and is a licensed clinical psychologist.
Dr. John Christy holds a degree in Atmospheric Sciences from the University of Illinois in Urbana-Champaign.
