Obago, S. O. (2014) examined the effect of financial literacy on the management of personal finances among employees of commercial banks in Kenya.

The growth of payday lending from the late 1990s through 2021 was accelerated by the consolidation of smaller operations and the securing of loans from commercial banks, public pensions, and private equity funds.
Payday lending operations consolidated and secured funding from commercial banks, public pensions, and private equity funds, accelerating growth from the late 1990s through 2021.

Research by Heintz and Melendez found that individuals in same-sex romantic relationships may struggle to discuss safe sex due to decreased perception of control over sex, fear of violence, and unequal power distributions.

In the study by Heintz and Melendez regarding same-sex romantic relationships, approximately 50% of those who reported violence also reported forced sexual experiences, with only half of that group reporting the use of safe-sex measures.
Heintz and Melendez concluded that sexual assault and abuse in same-sex romantic relationships are major concerns for HIV/AIDS infection because they decrease the frequency of safe-sex practices.
People in same-sex romantic relationships face special obstacles in dealing with domestic violence issues, which some researchers have labeled the double closet.
The research paper titled 'Ultrasonic Deep Brain Neuromodulation in Acute Disorders of Consciousness: A Proof-of-Concept' was published in the journal Brain Sciences.

Al-Rekabi et al. (2019) published research in Acta Biomaterialia titled 'Electrophysiological-mechanical coupling in the neuronal membrane and its role in ultrasound neuromodulation and general anaesthesia,' which explores the relationship between mechanical and electrical properties in neuronal membranes.
Groucutt et al. (2021) analyzed multiple hominin dispersals into Southwest Asia occurring over the past 400,000 years.
Wright (1991) studied the origins and development of ground stone assemblages in Late Pleistocene Southwest Asia.
State and non-state actors are influencing events in the Black Sea, South West Asia, and South East Asia.
Aysha Khan authored the article 'Changing global order and power realignments in Southwest Asia (ECO Region)', published in Margalla Papers in 2022, volume 26, issue 1, pages 61–73.

The proteostasis network (PN) is a macromolecular system that maintains proteome homeostasis by coordinating protein synthesis, folding, disaggregation, and degradation to support organismal health and longevity.
Proteostasis network (PN) component aggregation during ageing can elicit aberrant transcriptional procedures, reduced folding capacity, and the accumulation of misfolded species.

Chalcone-based derivatives, including nordihydroguaiaretic acid (NDGA), curcumin, resveratrol, quercetin, and isoliquiritigenin, act as dual-targeting drugs that can target both amyloid aggregations and the proteostasis network in vivo.

Lipid assemblies act as scaffolding for the construction and function of signaling complexes and play critical roles in the preservation of proteostasis, influencing ageing and neurodegenerative disease development.
4-Phenylbutyric Acid (4-PBA) elevates proteostasis ability in the face of misfolding-induced stress by activating the heat shock response (HSR) and the UPRmt pathway.
Proteostasis maintenance mechanisms include the proper folding of proteins and their degradation via proteasomes or lysosomes.
Autophagy contributes to proteostasis and affects the degradation of non-protein macromolecules, entire organelles, and invading pathogens.
Proteostasis is impaired during skeletal muscle aging, as reported in the 2019 paper 'Impaired proteostasis during skeletal muscle aging' published in Free Radical Biology and Medicine.
The relationship between proteostasis, oxidative stress, and aging is reviewed in the 2017 paper 'Proteostasis, oxidative stress and aging' published in Redox Biology.

Lipid assemblies are at the intersection of aging, proteostasis, and neurodegeneration, as described in the 2019 paper 'Lipid assemblies at the crossroads of aging, proteostasis, and neurodegeneration' published in Trends in Cell Biology.
Kolb et al. (2015) investigated the therapeutic effects of 4-phenylbutyric acid in maintaining proteostasis.

Labbadia J and Morimoto RI published 'The Biology of Proteostasis in Aging and Disease' in the Annual Review of Biochemistry in 2015, detailing the role of proteostasis in the aging process and associated diseases.

Lee TT, Chen P, Su MP, Li JC, Chang YW, Liu RW, et al. authored 'Loss of Fis1 impairs proteostasis during skeletal muscle aging in Drosophila', published in Aging Cell in 2021 (20:e13379, doi: 10.1111/acel.13379).
Information cannot be measured like physical properties such as mass, electric charge, or length, but can only be estimated or computed based on context and knowledge of the matter.
Each uterine tube is approximately 10 cm in length and 1 cm in diameter.
Professional development for remote workforces can be achieved through online education and high-level coursework offered by institutions via immersive platforms.
Businesses are encouraged to prioritize continuous learning through online education and high-level coursework on immersive platforms to future-proof their distributed workforce.
Geert Hofstede added the fifth dimension, Long-Term Orientation versus Short-Term Orientation, to his cultural dimensions theory after examining Chinese values.
Geert Hofstede’s cultural dimensions theory was expanded to include the fifth dimension of Long-Term Orientation versus Short-Term Orientation following his examination of Chinese values.
Shaygannia et al. (2015) published a review study in the Journal of Evidence-Based Complementary & Alternative Medicine regarding the pharmacological properties of Punica granatum L. (pomegranate).

S. Al-Majmaie, A.M. Alwan, S. Nath, et al. published an investigation into the antimicrobial activity of extracts from Aloe vera leaf gel, Punica granatum, Ricinus communis, and Musa species in the AIP Conference Proceedings in 2023.

Punicalagin derived from Punica granatum exhibits efficacy against the influenza A virus with an IC50 of 8.5 μg/mL by interacting with hemagglutinin and inhibiting viral adhesion to sialic acid receptors.
Punicalagins and ellagic acid, bioactive compounds found in Punica granatum (pomegranate), are effective against the MDR microorganism Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans.

A study (referenced as 269) examined the synergistic antibacterial properties of ethanolic extracts from Punica granatum (pericarp), Commiphora molmol, and Azadirachta indica (bark) combined with standard antibiotics (amoxicillin, metronidazole, tetracycline, and azithromycin) against periodontopathic bacteria (Porphyromonas gingivalis, Tannerella forsythia, Treponema denticola, and Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans).
The most effective synergistic antibacterial action observed in the study (269) was between Punica granatum extract and amoxicillin against Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans.
Deflation risk increases bond attractiveness and defensive positioning.
Weak consumer sentiment leads to cautious consumer behavior and defensive positioning.

Evaluation metrics for AI systems include fluency (measured by MAUVE), correctness (measured by EM recall for ASQA, recall-5 for QAMPARI, and claim recall for ELI5), and citation quality (measured by citation recall and citation precision).

The ASQA, QAMPARI, and ELI5 datasets are question-answering datasets characterized by containing factual questions where references are important, requiring long-text answers covering multiple aspects, and necessitating the synthesis of multiple sources.

During the 1990s, educational practices in early childhood classrooms shifted as free play and center time were replaced with rigid, skills-focused, and highly teacher-scripted curricula, particularly in schools serving underserved communities.

During the 1990s, educational practices in early childhood classrooms shifted as free play and center time were replaced with rigid, skills-focused, and teacher-scripted curricula, particularly in schools serving underserved communities.

Understanding the interplay between circadian physiology and the organization of body physiology across the lifespan, including neural reorganization, hormonal expression, and stress levels, is crucial for identifying optimal intervention times for positive health impacts.

The paper 'Contribution of Circadian Physiology and Sleep Homeostasis to Age-related changes in human sleep' published in Chronobiology International in 2009 (Dijk et al.) analyzes how circadian physiology and sleep homeostasis contribute to changes in sleep patterns during aging.

LeBourgeois MK, Wright KP, LeBourgeois HB, and Jenni OG found that dissonance between parent-selected bedtimes and young children's circadian physiology influences nighttime settling difficulties in a 2013 study published in Mind, Brain, and Education.

Cajochen et al. (2011) published a study in the Journal of Applied Physiology demonstrating that evening exposure to light-emitting diodes (LED)-backlit computer screens affects circadian physiology and cognitive performance.

The first adversarial collaboration on consciousness forced theorists to make more tangible predictions and compelled experimentalists to develop new techniques, even though it did not succeed in eliminating any theories from the field.

The adversarial collaboration study on consciousness involved 256 human subjects who viewed suprathreshold stimuli for variable durations while neural activity was measured using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), magnetoencephalography (MEG), and electrocorticography (ECoG).
Approximately 80% of the global population depends on traditional herbal medicine systems as their primary source of healthcare.

The European Medicines Agency (EMA) has developed monographs and guidelines for traditional herbal medicines used within the European Union to standardize safety evaluations, as noted by Krishna et al. (2024).

A high-fat diet and intermittent fasting can alter serum lipid composition, as a high-fat diet likely enriches for circulating lipids that differ from those released from adipose tissue breakdown during intermittent fasting.

Circulating lipid availability increases under conditions of caloric restriction, the ketogenic diet, and Western diets, and the accompanying increased fatty acid oxidation provides insights into the convergence of phenotypes observed with these diets, as noted by Corton et al. (2004), Sikder et al. (2018), Steinhauser et al. (2018), and Rosenbaum et al. (2019).
The character and structure of an adaptation are connected to its function, as demonstrated by bird wings and feathers enabling flight.
Lattin et al. (2011) validated that elevated corticosterone in feathers correlates with corticosterone-induced decreased feather quality.

Şengül et al. (2015) identify typical environmental evaluation criteria for energy systems as emission levels for SO2, NOx, CO2, particulate emissions, non-methane volatile organic compounds, and land use requirements.

Environmental Sustainability in energy systems focuses on managing the negative impacts of energy production and use while magnifying positive ones, with key indicators including emission levels for SO2, NOx, CO2, particulate emissions, non-methane volatile organic compounds, land use, GHG emissions, environmental external costs, radionuclides external costs, and severe accidents.
The brain-body connection is manifested across local, reflex, and central regulation levels, according to the review "Brain-body physiology: Local, reflex, and central communication."
Homeostatic regulation occurs through local control, such as paracrine or autocrine responses, and reflex control, which involves the nervous and endocrine systems.

The Joint Science Academies' Statement from 2005 asserts that it is likely that most of the warming in recent decades can be attributed to human activities, which has already led to changes in the Earth's climate.
The Joint Science Academies' Statement (2005) asserts that warming in recent decades has already led to changes in the Earth's climate.

A larger middle class in developing countries will create demands for political reform, potentially leading to democratization or instability, particularly in nations where this class coexists with high youth unemployment and weak, corrupt political institutions.
Iran's complex relations with the United States and Israel, characterized by Iranian doubt regarding the sustainability of agreements, exacerbate the risks associated with political reform.

family.name: U.S. indictment of five Chinese hackers May 19, 2014

aliases: U.S. indictment of five Chinese hackers May 2014
U.S. indictment of five Chinese hackers May 19 2014

facts.content: A U.S. court indicted five Chinese hackers, allegedly linked to the People’s Liberation Army, on charges of stealing trade technology from U.S. companies on May 19, 2014.
The Chinese government suspended its cooperation in the U.S.-China cybersecurity working group in response to the May 2014 U.S. indictment of five Chinese hackers.
Blue light triggers the opening of ion channels in plant cell membranes by regulating the phototropin receptor, which facilitates the uptake and accumulation of mineral nutrients.
Sulfur-containing secondary metabolites in plants may enhance their antimicrobial activity by promoting the development of ion channels in the cell membrane.

Electromagnetic fields (EMFs) in the brain involve action potentials in neurons, ion channels that modulate neural firing, ephaptic coupling (where the firing of a neuron indirectly influences other neurons), and oscillatory waves at the population level.

At chemical synapses, a signal is propagated when a chemical transmitter, such as glutamate, is released at the presynaptic terminal via exocytosis, diffuses across the synaptic cleft, and binds to receptors at the postsynaptic membrane to open an ion channel.
Processing of sensory input by the brain is achieved through a combination of neural circuits and ion channels embedded in the cell membrane.
An action potential is caused by temporary changes in membrane permeability for diffusible ions, which causes ion channels to open and ions to decrease their concentration gradients.
Myelin sheaths increase membrane resistance by patching areas where ion channels would otherwise leak.

In the Hodgkin and Huxley model, the action potential is presented as the result of depolarization-induced, voltage- and time-dependent opening and closure of ion channels in the membrane, allowing the passive flow of sodium (Na+) and potassium (K+) ions into and out of the neuronal cytoplasm along their respective electrochemical ion gradients.

The Hodgkin and Huxley model treats the neuronal membrane as a capacitor and ion channels as resistors, where changes in ionic conductance across the membrane cause a sudden and transient alteration of the transmembrane potential.

In the Hodgkin and Huxley model, ion channels open and close in a time- and voltage-dependent manner, allowing ionic currents (such as Na+, K+, and Cl-) to flow across the axonal membrane, generating a transient alteration of the transmembrane potential known as the action potential, which propagates via active and passive conduction.

The Hodgkin-Huxley model provides a framework for investigating the structural and functional properties of ion channels, specifically regarding the mechanisms of ion permeation, gating, and selectivity.

Wnek (2016) suggests that ion channels and pumps in the axolemma control coupled ion exchange events to ensure the monovalent/divalent balance is set correctly for a phase transition between resting and excited states.

Xu et al. (2013) reported that Na+ ion channels are distributed in axons in a periodic pattern that correlates with cytoskeletal rings of actin and spectrin, indicating a direct functional or structural interaction between these cytoskeletal filaments and membrane-anchored ion channel proteins.

The electricity-centered framework for modeling action potentials uses a reductionistic/mechanistic approach, focuses on electric phenomenology (ionic current flow), emphasizes functional transformations in microscopic cellular entities like ion channels, treats non-electrical phenomena as epiphenomena, and views propagation as an irreversible, energy-consuming process.

Ion channels in the neuronal membrane are gated by second messengers, neurotransmitters, or voltage changes, allowing permeable ions to flow down their electrochemical gradients and alter the membrane potential.

The concept of the 'ion channel' initially served as a designation for the relative contribution of ions, specifically sodium (Na+) and potassium (K+), to action potential generation and propagation, rather than denoting an actual physical mechanism.

The concept of the 'ion channel' within the original Hodgkin-Huxley model evolved from a largely theoretical concept into a recognized molecular entity in the decades following the model's formulation.
Ion channels and pumps are expressed in the early embryonic stages of chick and Xenopus development.
Specific ion channels play functional roles in development, cancer, and regeneration.

Masuelli, S., Real, S., McMillen, P., Oudin, M., Levin, M., and Roqué, M. published 'The Yin and Yang of Breast Cancer: Ion Channels as Determinants of Left-Right Functional Differences' in the International Journal of Molecular Sciences in 2023.

Cervera et al. (2020) published 'Bioelectrical model of head-tail patterning based on cell ion channels and intercellular gap junctions' in Bioelectrochemistry, which models head-tail patterning using ion channels and gap junctions.
Ion channels, membrane voltage, and the cell cycle serve as bioelectric controls of cell proliferation, according to a 2009 study by Blackiston, McLaughlin, and Levin published in Cell Cycle.
Ion channels are proteins that rapidly transport molecules across the cell membrane and are activated by changes in cell membrane voltage.
Bioelectricity arises at the cellular level from the movement of ions across cell membranes, which is driven by ion channels, pumps, and exchangers.
At the cellular level, bioelectricity manifests as voltage gradients across cell membranes, which are driven by the movement of ions through ion channels and pumps.

Deniz suggests that local changes in membrane potential could have important biological implications, such as influencing the function of ion channels and other membrane proteins regulated by voltage.

The publication 'From non-excitable single-cell to multicellular bioelectrical states supported by ion channels and gap junction proteins: electrical potentials as distributed controllers' by Whited and Levin (2019) describes how ion channels and gap junction proteins support bioelectrical states in both single-cell and multicellular organisms, acting as distributed controllers.

Sousounis, K., Erdogan, B., Levin, M., and Whited, J. L. published 'Precise control of ion channel and gap junction expression is required for patterning of the regenerating axolotl limb' in the 'International Journal of Developmental Biology' in 2020.

The publication 'EDEn – Electroceutical Design Environment: An Ion Channel Database with Small Molecule Modulators' by Durant et al. (2019) introduces the Electroceutical Design Environment (EDEn), a database of ion channels and their small molecule modulators.

The paper 'Systematizing cellular complexity: A Hilbertian approach to biological problems' explores cellular mechanisms, specifically homeostatic configuration, ion channels, and the harnessing of noise, to illustrate complex concepts and theories.

The authors of the paper 'A Hilbertian approach to biological problems' propose using cellular adaptability, ion channels, noise, and stochasticity as prototypical examples to illustrate a Hilbertian approach to biological problems.
The steady-state distribution of ion channels in cells is governed by a correlated homeostatic regulation.
Macroscopic electrogenic function in cells can be maintained despite variation in the types of ion channels present.

The co-regulation of ion channels through homeostatic mechanisms allows different channels to compensate for each other to maintain stability and manage the complex interplay between multiple properties to ensure robust macroscopic cellular function.
Intrinsic membrane excitability can be altered through dynamic epigenetic modification of DNA, specifically through methylation, which controls the expression of ion channels and synaptic response.
Ion channels are essential for ion transport across cell membranes and exhibit both phenotypic stability and plasticity, which are crucial for cellular adaptation.
Ion channels control membrane potential and intracellular ion concentration in both unicellular and multicellular organisms.
The diversity and complexity of ion channels contribute to the ability of complex cells to revisit optimal functional states quickly.

Early ion channels, such as calcium channels, evolved to include potassium and sodium ions, eventually becoming dedicated potassium and sodium channels to improve the precision and speed of signaling.
The evolution of efficient signaling mechanisms via ion channels was crucial for the development of multicellular organisms.
Eukaryotic cells possess a vast array of ion channels that facilitate complex cellular behaviors, allowing for enhanced efficiency and adaptability in responding to environmental changes.

In specialized excitable cells like neurons, the homeostatic regulation of ion channels is critical for maintaining excitability and compensating for perturbations such as channel deletions, mutations, or environmental disturbances.
Neurons adjust their intrinsic properties through activity-dependent mechanisms that sense and respond to changes in physiological activity via ion channels.
Ion channels act as critical mediators between extracellular events and intracellular processes by coupling signals from the environment to cytoplasmic biochemical pathways.

Mathematical models and simulations of ion channels, including traditional methods like Hodgkin–Huxley and Markov models, as well as modern techniques such as artificial neural networks (ANNs) and neural ordinary differential equations (ODEs), are used to model ion channel conductance and kinetics.
Ligand-receptors and ion channels are the primary molecular systems through which a cell communicates and samples the external world.
Patch clamp technology allows researchers to observe conductance fluctuations of individual ion channels as they oscillate between open and closed states due to thermal noise.

Additive noise linearly changes the activation barriers of ion channels with applied transmembrane voltage, which increases the instantaneous rate of the thermally activated reaction and exhibits stochastic resonance.
Harnessing stochastic resonance is an energy-efficient strategy for cells, as increasing the density of ion channels to combat noise is energy-consuming.
The amplitude of membrane potential fluctuation is determined by the number of ion channels present.
Large clusters of ion channels exhibit a type of stochastic resonance that occurs even in the presence of suboptimal intrinsic noise from single channels.
Ligand receptors and ion channels function as quantizing sampling devices, where their stochastic kinetics and density define the collective sampling rate.
Stochastic Resonance (SR) allows cells to modulate the sampling precision and information transfer of ion channels through a quantization operation.
Ion channels and excitable membranes are examples of thresholded dynamical systems that can be interpreted as multistable systems.
Signal transduction through ion channels involves a finite range and a discrete, stochastic sampling process, which can lead to signal distortion or loss of detail.
Stochastic Resonance (SR) affects the responsiveness of individual ion channels by causing them to open stochastically at different rates.
The presence of noise induces concentrated transitions to the open state in clusters of ion channels, rather than requiring a fixed threshold for all channels to switch to the open state.
Maffeo C, Bhattacharya S, Yoo J, Wells D, Aksimentiev A authored 'Modeling and Simulation of Ion Channels', published in Chem Rev in 2012, volume 112, issue 12, pages 6250–6284.
The duration of an ion channel's inactivity influences its reactivation, which modulates the channel's response to pH changes.

M. Wink authored the chapter 'Interference of alkaloids with neuroreceptors and ion channels' in the book 'Studies in Natural Products Chemistry', volume 21, edited by Atta-ur-Rahman and published by Elsevier in 2000.

Homeostatic science is generally designed to isolate local factors to discover 'silver bullets,' often failing to account for complex environments that favor anticipatory variations in functional set points or the integrative, top-down role of the brain.

When biological data varies between subjects, the homeostatic paradigm guides researchers to search for differences in local factors such as genes or gene expression, whereas the Predictive Adaptive Organization (PAO) paradigm searches for differences in environmental and top-down neural regulatory influences.
Threat actors conduct DDoS attacks using third-party underground services known as 'stressers' for rent, specifically Cosmic Network (V2), SpaceStresser, TheFlashDDOS, and EliteStresser.
Pro-Iranian hacktivist groups utilize third-party underground 'stressers' for rent, specifically Cosmic Network (V2), SpaceStresser, TheFlashDDOS, and EliteStresser, to conduct DDoS attacks.

Taheri et al. observed that homogeneity and the degree of entanglement between fibers are significant factors in defining the mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties of cellulose nanofibril and ordinary Portland cement composites.

Functionalists often use analogies between inter-level relations, such as the relationship between biological and biochemical properties or chemical and atomic properties, to explain how physical states realize conscious mental types.
Jeffrey Epstein met Harvard researcher Moshe Hoffman in Martin Nowak's lab in 2013.
Jeffrey Epstein arranged for an assistant to take Moshe Hoffman to a Broadway show during a visit to New York.

In February 2019, Moshe Hoffman emailed Jeffrey Epstein's assistant asking if Jeffrey Epstein could host him and friends at an apartment during a visit, despite noting "bad press" regarding Jeffrey Epstein.

Jeffrey Epstein's assistant emailed Jeffrey Epstein regarding Moshe Hoffman: “I get upset that he doesn’t find a way to check on you when he sees press and then gets in touch when he needs something.”
Moshe Hoffman, a lecturer at Harvard University, declined to comment on his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.

Important questions remain regarding the implementation of the second phase of the Comprehensive Plan, specifically concerning the authority and terms of reference of the Board of Trustees (BoP), the mandate and deployment timeline for the International Security Force (ISF), and the pathway for transferring governing authority to the Palestinian Authority (PA).

China and Russia abstained from voting on resolution 2803, citing reservations regarding the framework's lack of clarity on the role of the BoP, the scope of the ISF’s mandate, the timeline for a full Israeli withdrawal, and the pathway to restored Palestinian governance and statehood.

The regress argument in epistemology posits that there are two kinds of justified beliefs: basic beliefs, which begin a series of justified beliefs, and non-basic beliefs, which are based on other justified beliefs.
The regress argument assumes that the basing relation of justification is linear, meaning one belief is based on one or more other beliefs in an asymmetrical fashion.

The regress argument for foundationalism posits that for any justified belief (B1), if it is not basic, it must be justified by another belief (B2), which in turn requires justification, leading to an infinite chain or a loop unless the chain terminates in a basic belief.
The regress argument concludes that if justified beliefs exist, there must be basic beliefs that do not derive their justification from other beliefs.

The regress argument, even if sound, only demonstrates the necessity of doxastic basicality, which dependence coherentism allows for, meaning the argument only effectively defends experiential foundationalism against doxastic coherentism.

The regress argument asserts that if a chain of justification does not terminate in a basic belief, it must either loop back to the original belief or continue infinitely, both of which are considered unacceptable.

The regress argument for foundationalism only demonstrates the necessity of doxastic basicality, which is compatible with dependence coherentism, rather than proving the necessity of independence foundationalism.

Interdisciplinarity, as noted by Mariscal et al. (2019), reinforces the need for an adaptive ethical framework that integrates precaution, innovation, and social dialogue in the study of the origin of life.

Jeong-Oh Kim's course 'Radiant Intertextuality' operates on the premise that complex world problems require approaches from multiple angles, moving beyond interdisciplinarity as a mere rhetorical slogan to an actual practice.

Jeong Oh Kim argues that interdisciplinarity is often treated as a rhetorical slogan rather than an actual practice, where it is reduced to an additive list of disciplines rather than a transformative challenge.
Sulfur-reducing bacteria can utilize elemental sulfur instead of oxygen, reducing the sulfur to hydrogen sulfide.
Astrobiologist Dirk Schulze-Makuch has suggested hydrogen sulfide as a possible solvent for life on Jupiter's moon Io, where it is plentiful and may exist in liquid form below the surface.
Hydrogen sulfide is a chemical analog of water, though it is less polar and a weaker inorganic solvent.
Life forms utilizing hydrogen sulfide as a solvent might use a mixture of carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide as a carbon source and produce sulfur monoxide as an analog to oxygen.
Hydrogen sulfide is a chemical analog of water, but it is less polar and a weaker inorganic solvent.
Hydrogen sulfide is the closest chemical analog to water, though it is less polar and a weaker inorganic solvent.
Hypothetical life forms in hydrogen sulfide oceans might use a mixture of carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide as a carbon source and produce sulfur monoxide as an analog to oxygen.
Hydrogen sulfide has a narrow liquid temperature range, which increases with increasing pressure, similar to the behavior of hydrogen cyanide and ammonia.

The constrained multi-objective Bayesian optimization algorithm COMBOO balances active learning of the level-set defined on multiple unknowns with multi-objective optimization within the feasible region.

LLM4EA (Chen S. et al., 2024) aligns Knowledge Graphs using Large Language Model-generated annotations, employing active learning to reduce annotation space and a label refiner to correct noisy labels.

Inaccurate Named Entity Recognition and Relation Extraction prompting results can be corrected through active learning techniques (Wu et al., 2022) or by distilling large Pre-trained Language Models into smaller models for specific tasks (Agrawal et al., 2022).

Qian et al. (2020) proposed a method for disambiguating entity names using non-annotated examples, Distant Supervision (DS) to generate pseudo labels, and active learning to address deep learning model data requirements, which involves ranking predictions by model confidence and involving users in labeling top and bottom elements.
Playful learning engages active learning that is developmentally appropriate and offers a more equitable way of engaging children by increasing access to participation.
Teachers who support children as active learners view them as explorers and discoverers who construct understanding by bringing prior knowledge into the learning experience.

The article 'Engaging students in education for sustainable development: the benefits of active learning, reflective practices and flipped classroom pedagogies' was published in the Journal of Cleaner Production in 2021 (Volume 325, article 129318).

A device can simultaneously implement an AND operation and an OR operation depending on the relabeling of voltage inputs and outputs, as illustrated in research by Sprevak (2010) and Lee et al. (2021).
The determination of whether a logical gate implements an AND or OR operation depends on the downstream user of that gate.
Mozilla uses the Mozilla Public License (MPL) for its software releases, including the Firefox web browser.
Individuals commonly use open source software for operating systems like Linux, browsers like Firefox, media tools, office suites like LibreOffice, personal projects, and learning tools.
Well-known examples of open source software include GNU/Linux, Firefox (provided by the Mozilla Foundation), GIMP, jQuery, Java, Python, Git, PyTorch, and WordPress.
Juliette Bryant reports that at Teterboro airport, passports and luggage were not checked before boarding Jeffrey Epstein's airplane.
Juliette Bryant was driven directly onto the runway at Teterboro Airport without going through customs before a flight to the Caribbean.
Flight logs indicate that Jeffrey Epstein’s jets used Teterboro Airport hundreds of times.
Juliette Bryant stated: “I thought I’d been booked for my first shoot. A car drove me straight onto the runway at Teterboro Airport without going through customs.”
Jeffrey Epstein was arrested upon landing at Teterboro Airport in New Jersey on charges related to sex trafficking.
Mondia whitei is used for the treatment of erectile dysfunction, and Cocos nucifera is used for the treatment of vision problems.
Radiation therapy to the head or eye, as well as chemotherapy, targeted therapies, immunotherapies, and hormone therapies, can cause vision changes.
Patients who experience vision changes from cancer treatment should consult their health care team about seeing an ophthalmologist.
Radiation to the head or eye, chemotherapy, targeted therapies, immunotherapies, and hormone therapies can cause vision changes, including cataracts and glaucoma, in cancer survivors.
Cancer survivors experiencing vision changes from treatment should consult an ophthalmologist.
Reuben and the 'Interview with E.D.' crew traveled to Sidney, Nebraska, to meet Beverly Rickard and document a fourth regression session conducted by Suzanne Spooner.

In the session 'The RA Files: Part 4 - Operating from the Heart,' recorded on June 12th, 2023, the individual Reuben discusses with the entity RA topics including the connection between RA and the sun, the energetic influence of solar flares, Earth's shifting magnetic field, cataclysmic cycles, the illusion of linear time, and vibrational shifts affecting human evolution.

In a session titled 'The RA Files: Part 3 - Splitting Timelines,' an individual named Reuben engages with an entity called RA to discuss esoteric topics including communication through animals, the role of higher vibrational beings in human affairs, the energetic structure of reality, ancient gods, hierarchical distortions in spiritual traditions, and the concept of a 'splitting of worlds' where different vibrational states create separate experiences.

The podcast episode titled 'THE RA FILES: PART 2 - Bridge to God' features Reuben exploring topics such as the origins of ancient gods, the intelligence of the All, and the influence of vibrational frequencies on human evolution.

In 'The RA Files: Part 8 - The Call', Reuben engages in a conversation with an entity named RA regarding topics including energetic influences on Donald Trump, global consciousness, collective prayer, extraterrestrial involvement in Earth's affairs, the Space Force, and groups working for justice.

In 'The RA Files: Part 8 - The Call', Reuben engages in a conversation with an entity identified as RA regarding topics including energetic influences on Donald Trump, global consciousness, collective prayer, extraterrestrial involvement in Earth's affairs, the Space Force, and groups working for justice.
Reuben hosts a series of conversations titled "The RA Files," with Part 7 featuring a discussion with an entity identified as RA regarding global figures and conflicts.

The Gorno-Badakhshan region of Tajikistan serves as a case study for how cultural differences between groups can lead to security-related conflict, as the area's specific ethnic and linguistic makeup triggers persistent disagreements between central authorities and residents.

In May 2022, confrontations in the Gorno-Badakhshan region of Tajikistan, triggered by the detention of local leaders, resulted in dozens of casualties and illustrated how perceived persecution by authorities can incite violent behavior among marginalized groups.

The Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model, grounded in environmental psychology, posits that external stimuli (S) such as website visuals and interactions impact a consumer's internal state (O) of emotions and thoughts, which subsequently leads to a behavioral response (R) of making an impulsive purchase.
Frictionless navigation in e-commerce, achieved through simple layouts and quick-loading pages, preserves user emotional momentum and reduces distractions that might prevent an impulsive purchase.
Chen et al. (2017) contend that consumers first experience an urge to make an impulsive purchase, and subsequently implement the behavior of an impulse purchase.

Osswald et al. (2012) and Bousbahi and Alrazgan (2015) found that customer anxiety can significantly impact whether a customer completes an impulsive purchase or becomes reluctant, regardless of the initial urge to buy.
Critics within Iran argue that hardline foreign policy analysts have steered the country into strategic isolation due to a lack of global experience and nuanced understanding.
Strategic isolation in Iran has emerged primarily due to systemic pressures rather than domestic developments alone, leading to a lack of meaningful alliances with major powers.

Historically, human clothing has evolved from animal skins for weather protection to the domestication of vegetable fibers such as cotton, linen, and hemp, and eventually to synthetic materials like nylon and polyester developed by the petrochemical industry in the last 100 years.
Polyester and nylon together account for approximately 70% of the synthetic materials used in the textile industry.
Elon Musk has publicly dismissed extraterrestrial claims, asserting that Starlink satellites would detect alien activity.

Elon Musk has publicly dismissed extraterrestrial claims, asserting that his Starlink satellites would detect alien activity, a stance Steven Greer attributes to Musk’s vested interest in maintaining control over energy and aerospace markets.
Houston, Texas, is characterized by unbridled growth, land speculation, and a lack of zoning, which leads to ad hoc developments scattered across the landscape, as described in Houston Genetic City.

Descriptivism suggests that a name is an abbreviation of a description (a set of properties) and secures its reference through a process of property fitting, where the object that best fits the description is the referent.
Saul Kripke argued in 'Naming and Necessity' that a name does not secure its reference via description fitting, but rather through a historical-causal link tracing back to the process of naming.

Saul Kripke posits that a name does not have a sense, or at least not a sense rich enough to determine reference, and that a name is a rigid designator that refers to the same object in all possible worlds.

David Chalmers explains that reference is determined by distinguishing between epistemic possibilities, represented by primary intension, and metaphysical necessities, represented by secondary intension.
The Mozilla Public License (MPL) version 2.0 was released in 2012 and the Eclipse Public License (EPL) version 2 was released in 2017.

The Open Source Initiative (OSI) particularly recommends seven approved licenses as popular, widely used, or having strong communities: Apache License 2.0, BSD 3-Clause and BSD 2-Clause Licenses, all versions of the GNU General Public License, all versions of the GNU Lesser Public License, MIT License, Mozilla Public License 2.0, Common Development and Distribution License (CDDL), and Eclipse Public License version 2.0.

Javier Garcia-German's book 'Thermodynamic Interactions: Architectural Exploration into Material, Physiological and Territorial Atmospheres' argues that energy and sustainability are complex topics that require addressing core disciplinary values alongside ideas from other fields of knowledge.

Javier Garcia-German's book 'Thermodynamic Interactions' proposes that architecture must be overlaid with disciplines such as meteorology, thermodynamics, and physiology to engage with the environment, built structures, and the human body in a holistic way.

The book 'Thermodynamic Interactions' by Javier Garcia-German is structured into three blocks: Territorial Atmospheres, which covers the interaction between the environment and built structures; Material Atmospheres, which covers the interaction between a building and the climate it generates; and Physiological Atmospheres, which covers the interaction between indoor ambient conditions and the physiological and psychological effects on human beings.
H. Kent Baker and Victor Ricciardi authored the book 'Investor Behavior—The Psychology of Financial Planning and Investing', published in 2014 by John Wiley & Sons in Hoboken, NJ.

Victor Ricciardi is an assistant professor of financial management at Goucher College in Baltimore, Maryland, where he teaches financial planning, investments, corporate finance, behavioral finance, and the psychology of money.
Victor Ricciardi authored the article 'Our 3-Year Market Hangover,' published in MoneySense in 2012.

Victor Ricciardi and Douglas Rice authored the chapter 'Risk Perception and Risk Tolerance' in the 2014 book 'Investor Behavior—The Psychology of Financial Planning and Investment,' edited by H. Kent Baker and Victor Ricciardi.

Modern warehouses are defined by complex interactions between resources (personnel, equipment), processes (receiving, storage, picking, packing, shipping), and physical layouts.

The simulation captures package-specific data, including a unique package ID and timestamps for pickup from the supplier, waiting time at the transfer point, loading and departure time with an AGV, arrival time at the storage block, and final placement into storage.

The proposed framework utilizes a custom Knowledge Graph (KG) schema where resources such as suppliers, workers, AGVs, forklifts, and storage are represented as nodes, while the movement of packages between these resources is represented as edges. Operational data, including timestamps, is incorporated as features of these nodes and edges, with the KG constructed from output logs generated by a Discrete Event Simulation (DES) model.

The knowledge graph schema for the warehouse simulation defines nodes for SUPPLIER (properties: supplier_id, arrival_time, discharge_start, discharge_end), WORKER (property: worker_id), AGV (property: agv_id), FL/Forklift (property: forklift_id), and STORAGE (property: block_id).

Forklift FL_00 is identified as the primary bottleneck in the discharge flow, with an average waiting time of approximately 333 seconds and a package movement time from AGVs to storage of 152 seconds, which is 29 seconds above the global average.
The proposed method identified that Forklift FL_00 had a package movement time from AGV to storage of 152 seconds, compared to a global average of 123 seconds.

The system supports queries regarding forklift operations, specifically identifying the most underutilized forklift, calculating the utilization rate (percentage of time in use), determining the average time taken to move a package to storage, and counting packages handled by each forklift.

The system supports queries regarding package logistics, specifically identifying storage blocks with the highest number of containers, calculating average discharge times, determining average waiting times for transfers between AGVs and forklifts, and identifying packages with the longest total transit time from dock arrival to final storage.

To maintain grid stability in a system with very high levels of intermittent generation, Severin Borenstein and Ryan Kellogg propose a combination of four approaches: long-distance power transmission, storage, demand flexibility, and dispatchable generation.

The analysis evaluates wind, solar, and storage costs using two scenarios: a reference scenario based on anticipated research and development, and a breakthrough scenario representing a 5% probability outcome.
Decarbonization strategies require adequate low-carbon electricity supply and often on-site renewables or storage to manage load, which can dramatically cut emissions by tapping into clean power.

To overcome the barrier of variability in renewable power output, the authors suggest solutions including better prediction of variability, strategies for reducing it, changes in the operation of power plants, reserves, transmission systems, and storage, improved planning of renewable capacity expansion, and implementation of new regulatory paradigms, rate structures, and standards.

Finding 5-7 of the National Academies report states that the variability of renewable generation does not prevent the future expansion of renewable technologies, although high penetration of renewables will likely require investments in innovation to improve grid technologies, storage, and regulatory paradigms.

The National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine report identifies three key requirements for energy systems: the encouragement of storage for renewable integration, the implementation of supportive and flexible regulatory regimes, and investment in re-architected, reliable, and efficient grids.

Community-scale renewable energy projects are expected to be combined over time with efficiency, demand response, microgrids, combined heat and power, storage, and other distributed energy approaches.

Solomon, Faiman, and Meron (2012) analyzed the role of conventional power plants in a grid fed mainly by photovoltaic energy and storage, including shadow capacity requirements, published in Energy Policy, volume 48(C), pages 479-486.

The global energy sector is undergoing a fast transition driven by the demand for sustainability, efficiency, and resilience, facilitated by technological developments in renewable energy efficiency and storage.

The 2004 'Pepsi vs. Coke' experiment by Montague et al. shifted the focus of decision-making research toward non-rational and emotionally driven aspects, challenging traditional assumptions in psychology and behavioral science.

Plants such as echinacea and elderberry, used by Native American tribes, are known for their immune-boosting properties.
Native American tribes, specifically the Cherokees and Lakotas, considered the Pleiades to be ancestors and sources of spiritual wisdom.
Native American tribes frequently took women and children as captives for adoption because they adapted more easily to new environments, and some tribes held captives as hostages for payment.

Various Native American tribes practiced debt slavery or imposed slavery on tribal members who committed crimes, with full tribal status restored once the enslaved individual worked off their obligations to the tribal society.

Upon contact with Europeans, Native American tribes began participating in the slave trade and attempted to use captives from enemy tribes as a method of playing one tribe against another in a divide and conquer strategy.

facts.content: William James authored 'The varieties of religious experience: A study in human nature', originally published in 1902.
William James authored 'The Varieties of Religious Experience: A Study in Human Nature'.
William James's 'The Varieties of Religious Experience' and Andrew Newberg's 'Why God Won’t Go Away' are resources for the academic study of spiritual experience.

William James, often referred to as the 'father of American psychology,' established a naturalistic, psychological foundation for the study of religion through his lectures published as 'The Varieties of Religious Experience' (1902).

William James quotes St. Paul in 'The Varieties of Religious Experience' (1902) regarding a Christian transcendent encounter: “I live, yet not I, but Christ liveth in me. Only when I become as nothing can God enter in and no difference between his life and mine remain outstanding.”

The working paper 'The Macroeconomic Effects of Tariffs: Evidence From U.S. Historical Data' contributes to econometrics and economic history literature by using a structured, narrative-based method to identify tariff policy decisions driven by factors unrelated to economic conditions.
Econometrics is defined as the application of statistical methods to the analysis of economic data.
Turmeric (Curcuma longa) is a yellow spice used in Indian cuisine and traditional Ayurvedic medicine that contains the active compound curcumin.
In Indian Ayurvedic medicine, the plant Murraya koenigii is referred to as “krishnanimba” and has been used in a variety of ways for centuries.
Ayurvedic and Unani medicine systems utilize Ocimum basilicum to treat various physiological and lifestyle-related afflictions.

Traditional Chinese Medicine, Ayurvedic medicine, and African traditional medicine systems represent millennia of accumulated knowledge regarding the therapeutic properties and clinical applications of botanical compounds.
Ayurvedic medicine focuses on equilibrium, preventive, and personalized care, which ensures its sustained significance in holistic and integrative healthcare methodologies.
Ayurvedic medicine provides insights for current healthcare professionals regarding the relationships among mind, body, and environment to address complex health issues.
Ayurvedic medicine influences contemporary discourse on preventive medicine, individualized therapy, and the integration of traditional and modern therapeutic methodologies.
Medicinal plants serve as the principal source of therapeutic chemicals and the foundation of Ayurvedic medicine.

In Ayurvedic medicine, food-based nutrition is considered crucial for bodily growth, and the five fundamental elements are the primary source of sustenance for body parts after the action of bio-fire (Agni).
Ayurvedic medicine classifies body tissues as structural entities and humors as physiological entities, both of which originate from combinations of the five essential components.
Botanical resources and plant-derived formulations are the basis for preserving health balance and treating diseases in Ayurvedic medicine.

Ayurvedic medicine is one of the world's oldest systematized approaches to medicinal plant utilization, providing a framework for understanding how botanical compounds interact with human physiology to promote health and treat disease.

Ayurvedic medicine has catalogued over 7,000 medicinal plants and developed methodologies for plant preparation and application that inform modern phytotherapy research, according to Anand et al. (2022).
Ayurvedic medicine demonstrates the healing properties of plants and indicates that traditional approaches remain relevant despite the introduction of antibiotics.

Approximately 300 million people globally collect products from forests, including fruits, leafy vegetables, nuts, seeds, and edible oils, as reported by Bharuch and Pretty (2010) and Belcher et al. (2005).
Minor edible plant parts utilized by the local population include tubers (2.83%), bulbs (1.89%), epicarp (1.89%), additional seeds (1.89%), nuts (0.94%), and roots (0.94%).

Other plant parts used by the Quitexe people for medicinal purposes include inflorescences (3.19%), stems (3.19%), bulbs (2.13%), corn hair (1.06%), stones (1.06%), root bark (1.06%), nuts (1.06%), resin (1.06%), rhizomes (1.06%), and tubers (1.06%).
The dietary use of wild fruits, nuts, seeds, and leaves is documented in numerous historical records.
Human ancestral forms consumed a varied diet consisting of fiber, starch-rich foods, meats, nuts, and fruits for a long period.
The Paleolithic diet typically includes vegetables, fruits, nuts, roots, and meat, while excluding dairy products, grains, sugar, legumes, processed oils, salt, alcohol, and coffee.
Permissible foods in the Paleolithic diet include vegetables, fruits, nuts, roots, meat, and organ meats.

The Paleolithic diet includes vegetables (including root vegetables), fruit (including fruit oils like olive, coconut, and palm oil), nuts, fish, meat, and eggs, while excluding dairy, grain-based foods, legumes, extra sugar, and industrial nutritional products like refined fats and carbohydrates.
The Paleolithic diet avoids processed foods and emphasizes the consumption of vegetables, fruits, nuts, seeds, eggs, and lean meats.

A diet revolving around meat and dairy will take a greater toll on global resources than a diet revolving around unrefined grains, nuts, fruits, and vegetables, which is a significant concern given the global population is expected to reach nine billion by 2050.
The 'paleo diet' recommends consuming meat, fish, fruits, vegetables, eggs, and nuts, while excluding cereal grains, dairy products, legumes, refined sugar, and processed foods.

Researchers argue that meat-eating was incorporated into a mixed diet, which potentially allowed for a shift toward the consumption of energy-rich plant resources such as seeds, nuts, and underground storage organs.

Neanderthals across Eurasia consumed a range of plant foods and fungi, including fruits, nuts, wild grass seeds, legumes, underground storage organs (USOs), and mushrooms, as evidenced by microfossils and eukaryotic aDNA preserved in dental calculus.
Goren-Inbar et al. (2002) studied nuts, nut-cracking behavior, and the use of pitted stones at the Gesher Benot Ya‘aqov site in Israel.
The Paleo Diet encourages the consumption of healthy fats, primarily from sources such as avocados, nuts, seeds, and olive oil.

The Fruit and Vegetable Consumption indicator measures the daily intake of fruits, vegetables, pulses, nuts, and seeds in grams per capita per day, serving as a proxy for a healthy diet and specific micronutrient intakes.
Ancient diets were composed of phytonutrients found in grains, native plants, mushrooms, fruits, legumes, nuts, honey, and seeds.

Plant-based diets are characterized by a high intake of vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes, and nuts, while minimizing or excluding animal products like red meat, fish, and dairy. This dietary pattern is high in fiber and nutrients and may support heart health, blood sugar management, and overall well-being.

The Mediterranean diet prioritizes whole, unprocessed foods such as fruits, vegetables, whole grains, beans, nuts, seeds, fish, and healthy fats like olive oil. This diet provides fiber, healthy fats, and antioxidants, and may support heart health, blood sugar management, and overall wellness.

The Mediterranean diet, which is associated with beneficial effects on cardiovascular health, comprises fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, and healthy fats such as olive oil and nuts, contrasting with the highly processed foods found in the Western diet.

Replacing high-fat and sugar products in the Western diet with fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, and whole grains may reduce low-grade inflammation and prevent the development of cardiovascular disease (CVD).

The Mediterranean Diet Score (MDS) ranges from 0 (minimal adherence) to 9 (maximal adherence) and is calculated based on nine components: vegetables, legumes, fruits and nuts, cereal, fish, the ratio of monounsaturated to saturated lipids, meat, dairy products, and alcohol.

The Mediterranean-Style Dietary Pattern Score (MSDPS) assigns points based on daily or weekly servings of specific food groups, including whole grains (8 servings/day), fruits (3 servings/day), vegetables (6 servings/day), dairy (2 servings/day), wine (1.5 servings/day for women, 3 servings/day for men), fish/seafood (6 servings/week), poultry (4 servings/week), olives/legumes/nuts (4 servings/week), potatoes/starchy roots (3 servings/week), eggs (3 servings/week), sweets (3 servings/week), and meat (1 serving/week).

The applicability of the Mediterranean Diet Score (MDS) is not established in regions where distinctive components like olive oil, red wine, nuts, legumes, or fish are unavailable or not part of cultural dietary patterns.

The Healthy Diet Indicator (HDI) scores dietary intake based on eleven components: fruits and vegetables (≥ 400g), beans and other legumes (> 0g), nuts and seeds (> 0g), whole grains (> 0g), dietary fiber (> 25g), total fat (< 30% total energy), saturated fat (< 10% total energy), dietary sodium (< 2g), free sugars (< 10% total energy), processed meat (0g), and unprocessed red meat (≤ 71g).
Global nut and seed intake increased by 17% between 2010 and 2018, starting from a very low baseline.

A quarter of all deaths among adults globally are attributable to poor diets, defined as diets low in fruits, vegetables, nuts, seeds, and whole grains, and high in red meat, processed meat, and sugary drinks.

Imbalanced diets low in fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, seeds, and whole grains, and high in red and processed meat are responsible for one of the greatest health burdens globally and in most regions.

A healthy diet consists of plenty of fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, seeds, whole grains, and oils high in unsaturated fats, while containing little to no red and processed meat, sugar-sweetened beverages, refined grains, and oils high in saturated fats.

The average fresh-water footprint per tonne of animal-based products is generally greater than that of plant-based products, with the exceptions of milk, which has a relatively low water footprint, and nuts, which have a relatively high water footprint when measured on a per-tonne basis but not on a per-calorie or per-protein basis.

The Global Nutrition Report 2021 analysis includes diet-disease associations for low intake of fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts/seeds, and whole grains, as well as for high intake of red meat, processed meat, and sugary drinks.

The EAT-Lancet Commission on Healthy Diets from Sustainable Food Systems established specific dietary recommendations for minimum intake of health-promoting foods (fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, whole grains) and maximum intake of foods with detrimental health or environmental impacts (red meat, processed meat, dairy, fish).

Diets globally continue to lack sufficient amounts of fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, and whole grains, while including excessive and sometimes rising amounts of red meat, processed meat, and sugary drinks.
The Western pattern diet is characterized by low intakes of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, fish, nuts, and seeds.

Suboptimal diets are characterized by low intake of fibers, fruits, vegetables, legumes, whole grains, nuts, seeds, milk, seafood, calcium, and healthy fats (omega-3 fatty acids, polyunsaturated fatty acids) and high intake of trans-fatty acids, sodium, red or processed meat, and sugar-sweetened beverages, according to Afshin et al. (2019).

The planetary health diet prioritizes the consumption of vegetables, fruits, legumes, whole grains, nuts, and fish, while limiting the intake of red meat and tubers, and promoting moderate consumption of eggs, poultry, and dairy products.
Pre-contact American Indian and Alaska Native diets included a wide variety of harvested foods such as seeds, nuts, corn, beans, chile, squash, wild fruits, greens, herbs, fish, and game.

The National Indian Council on Aging suggests snacking on whole grains, fruits, vegetables, nuts, and seeds.
There are large global shortfalls in the availability of fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, and seeds, as well as large disparities among regions regarding the use of animal source foods.
Fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, and seeds have received minimal agricultural research and development funding over the last 50 years.

A study on global food systems found substantial but inconsistent progress toward alignment with dietary guidelines, characterized by large global shortfalls in fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, and seeds, alongside large regional disparities in the use of animal source foods.
Latin America and the Caribbean display lower diet diversity and higher sodium consumption, despite higher intakes of legumes and nuts.
Average dietary intake across the studied regions was lower than minimum recommendations for whole-grain cereal products, dairy, legumes, fish, cheese, and nuts.
Respondents in Copenhagen reported the most frequent intake of vegetables, nuts, and whole-grain bread compared to the other four CSTs.
In 2018, the highest global AHEI component scores for healthier items were for legumes/nuts (5.0), followed by whole grains (4.7), seafood omega-3 fat (4.2), and non-starchy vegetables (3.9).
Globally, more educated individuals had lower AHEI scores (less favorable consumption levels) for red/processed meat (-0.6), sugar-sweetened beverages (-0.6), and nuts and legumes (-0.1).
Globally, urban residents had higher AHEI scores for fruit (+0.2) and whole grains (+0.2), but lower scores for sugar-sweetened beverages (-0.5), red/processed meat (-0.4), and legumes/nuts (-0.1).

Globally, between 1990 and 2018, AHEI scores increased for non-starchy vegetables (+1.1), legumes/nuts (+1.1), and fruit (+0.1); decreased for red/processed meat (−1.4), sugar-sweetened beverages (−0.6), and sodium (−0.4); and remained stable for whole grains (+0.1), PUFAs (0), and seafood omega-3 (0).

In low-income countries in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa, consumption of healthy components such as fruit, non-starchy vegetables, legumes/nuts, seafood omega-3 fat, and polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) remained far from optimal in 2018.

In high-income countries, Central/Eastern Europe, Central Asia, the Middle East, and Northern Africa, improvements in dietary quality driven by increased intake of fruit, non-starchy vegetables, legumes/nuts, and whole grains have been offset by stable or only minor reductions in red/processed meats, sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs), and sodium.

Foods that may help reduce inflammation include olive oil, high-fiber foods, tomatoes, nuts (such as walnuts and almonds), fatty fish (such as salmon and sardines), and fruits (including blueberries and oranges).

UT MD Anderson dietitians recommend reducing inflammation by eating meals rich in whole grains, vegetables, fruits, beans, nuts, seeds, complex carbohydrates, fiber, lean protein, and n-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, while limiting saturated fats and refined sugars.

To reduce chronic inflammation, individuals should: (1) eat anti-inflammatory foods such as whole grains, fruits, vegetables, fatty fish, nuts, and seeds; (2) reduce or remove packaged and ultra-processed foods containing saturated and trans fats; (3) consult a doctor regarding long-term use of antibiotics, antacids, and NSAIDs; (4) exercise regularly to maintain weight and support immune function; (5) sleep seven to eight hours to stimulate human growth hormones and testosterone; and (6) manage stress to maintain the body's ability to regulate inflammatory response.

To manage nutrition during the menstrual phase, individuals should consume iron-rich foods like spinach, red meat, beans, and nuts, paired with vitamin C-rich foods like oranges or broccoli to aid iron absorption.

In the energetics-based framework by McEwen and Wingfield (2003), Type I allostatic overload occurs when allostatic load exceeds the energetic resources available in the environment, while Type II allostatic overload occurs when allostatic load remains high without exceeding basal energy available.

In the energetics-based framework proposed by Bruce McEwen and John Wingfield (2003), allostatic load is calculated as the function: allostatic load = Ee (basal existence) + Ei (routine activity) + Eo (unpredictable perturbations).

The 2015 Shenzhen Biennale of Architecture and Urbanism (UABB) catalogue, titled 'Re-Living the City', was authored by Aaron Betsky, Alfredo Brillembourg, Hubert Klumpner, and Doreen Heng Liu.

Epistemic justification is considered a positive property of beliefs because justified beliefs originate from reliable processes that promote the goal of believing what is true rather than what is false.
Process reliabilism holds that a belief is justified if it is produced by a reliable process type, defined as a process that produces true beliefs more often than false beliefs.
In process reliabilism, vision is considered a reliable process type if the beliefs produced by vision are true more often than they are false across all times and places.
Process reliabilism is the view that justified beliefs are beliefs produced by a reliable process type.

Process reliabilism asserts that whether a person's belief is justified depends on contingent factors external to the person's mind, specifically whether the belief is produced by a reliable process type.
Outdoor play allows children to use all their senses to build skills like spatial awareness and balance, and can improve a child's attention span.
